file.content:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version-file: go.mod

      - name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: latest
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

  npm-publish:
    needs: release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - name: Get version from tag
        id: version
        run: echo "VERSION=${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Build npm packages
        run: |
          cd npm
          node build-packages.js ${{ steps.version.outputs.VERSION }}

      - name: Publish platform packages
        run: |
          for dir in npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 npm/devdash-darwin-x64 npm/devdash-linux-x64 npm/devdash-linux-arm64 npm/devdash-win32-x64 npm/devdash-win32-arm64; do
            if [ -d "$dir" ]; then
              cd "$dir"
              npm publish --access public || true
              cd ../..
            fi
          done
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

      - name: Publish wrapper package
        run: |
          cd npm/devdash-cli
          npm publish --access public
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

file.content:
#!/bin/sh
set -e

# DevDash CLI installer
# Usage: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh

REPO="jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go"
INSTALL_DIR="${DEVDASH_INSTALL_DIR:-$HOME/.local/bin}"
BINARY="devdash"

# Detect platform
OS="$(uname -s | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')"
ARCH="$(uname -m)"

case "$ARCH" in
  x86_64|amd64) ARCH="amd64" ;;
  arm64|aarch64) ARCH="arm64" ;;
  *)
    echo "Error: unsupported architecture: $ARCH"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

case "$OS" in
  darwin|linux) EXT="tar.gz" ;;
  mingw*|msys*|cygwin*|windows*) OS="windows"; EXT="zip" ;;
  *)
    echo "Error: unsupported OS: $OS"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

# Get latest version
echo "Fetching latest version..."
VERSION=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/latest" | grep '"tag_name"' | sed -E 's/.*"v([^"]+)".*/\1/')

if [ -z "$VERSION" ]; then
  echo "Error: could not determine latest version"
  exit 1
fi

echo "Installing devdash v${VERSION} (${OS}/${ARCH})..."

# Download
ARCHIVE="devdash_${VERSION}_${OS}_${ARCH}.${EXT}"
URL="https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/v${VERSION}/${ARCHIVE}"

if [ "$OS" = "windows" ]; then
  BINARY="devdash.exe"
fi

TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap 'rm -rf "$TMPDIR"' EXIT

echo "Downloading $URL..."
curl -fsSL "$URL" -o "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE"

# Extract
if [ "$EXT" = "zip" ]; then
  unzip -q "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE" -d "$TMPDIR"
else
  tar -xzf "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE" -C "$TMPDIR"
fi

# Install
mkdir -p "$INSTALL_DIR"
cp "$TMPDIR/$BINARY" "$INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"
chmod +x "$INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"

echo "Installed to $INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"

# Check PATH
case ":$PATH:" in
  *":$INSTALL_DIR:"*) ;;
  *)
    echo ""
    echo "Note: $INSTALL_DIR is not in your PATH."
    echo "Add it with:"
    echo "  export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\""
    echo ""
    SHELL_NAME=$(basename "$SHELL")
    case "$SHELL_NAME" in
      zsh)  echo "  echo 'export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\"' >> ~/.zshrc" ;;
      bash) echo "  echo 'export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\"' >> ~/.bashrc" ;;
      fish) echo "  fish_add_path $INSTALL_DIR" ;;
    esac
    ;;
esac

echo ""
echo "Run 'devdash version' to verify."

version: 2

before:
  hooks:
    - go mod tidy

builds:
  - id: devdash
    main: ./cmd/devdash
    binary: devdash
    env:
      - CGO_ENABLED=0
    goos:
      - darwin
      - linux
      - windows
    goarch:
      - amd64
      - arm64
    ldflags:
      - -s -w
      - -X github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/internal/commands.Version={{.Version}}

archives:
  - id: default
    format: tar.gz
    format_overrides:
      - goos: windows
        format: zip
    name_template: "devdash_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}"
    files:
      - LICENSE

checksum:
  name_template: "checksums.txt"

changelog:
  sort: asc
  filters:
    exclude:
      - "^docs:"
      - "^test:"
      - "^ci:"

release:
  github:
    owner: jasonmassey
    name: devdash-cli-go
  draft: false
  prerelease: auto

input.prompt:
I need a plan to get NPM binary distribution working for a Go CLI tool. The scaffolding exists but has never been published. I also need to verify the curl/bash installer works.

## Current State

**GoReleaser** (`.goreleaser.yml`):
- Builds for 6 platforms: darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, linux/arm64, windows/amd64, windows/arm64
- Archives named: `devdash_<version>_<os>_<arch>.tar.gz` (or .zip for windows)
- Binary name: `devdash`
- Build output: `dist/` directory, build ID is `devdash`
- GoReleaser v2 format

**NPM wrapper** (`npm/devdash-cli/package.json`):
- Scoped to `@devdashproject/devdash-cli`
- Has `"bin": { "devdash": "bin/devdash" }` — but **no actual bin/devdash file exists**
- optionalDependencies list 6 platform packages
- postinstall.js tries to resolve binary from optional dep, falls back to `npm install`

**NPM build script** (`npm/build-packages.js`):
- Reads GoReleaser output from `../dist/`
- Creates 6 platform packages under `npm/devdash-<platform>/`
- Each platform package gets: `package.json` with `os`/`cpu` fields, and `bin/devdash` binary
- Platform packages ALSO have `"bin": { "devdash": "bin/<binary>" }` entries
- Expects binary at `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash` or `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>_v1/devdash`

**Release workflow** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`):
- Job 1: GoReleaser builds + publishes GitHub release
- Job 2 (needs job 1): runs build-packages.js, publishes 6 platform packages + wrapper to npm
- Uses `NPM_TOKEN` secret

**Curl installer** (`install.sh`):
- Fetches latest release from GitHub API
- Downloads archive for detected platform
- Extracts and installs to `~/.local/bin`

## Issues I've Identified

1. **Missing bin shim in wrapper package**: The wrapper's `"bin": { "devdash": "bin/devdash" }` points to a file that doesn't exist. When npm installs, it needs something at that path. The standard pattern (used by esbuild, turbo, SWC) is a JavaScript launcher script that finds the platform-specific binary and spawns it.

2. **Platform packages shouldn't have bin entries**: If both the wrapper AND platform packages define `"bin": { "devdash": ... }`, npm might get confused. The pattern is: wrapper has the bin entry (with JS shim), platform packages just provide the raw binary without bin entries.

3. **GoReleaser output path uncertainty**: build-packages.js expects binaries at `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash`. GoReleaser v2 may use different directory naming. The script has a `_v1` fallback but this may not be correct. Need to verify actual output.

4. **NPM_TOKEN not configured**: The workflow uses `secrets.NPM_TOKEN` but the user has never published, so this likely isn't set up.

5. **Workflow doesn't download GoReleaser artifacts**: The npm-publish job checks out the code but doesn't have access to the `dist/` directory from the GoReleaser job. It needs to use `actions/upload-artifact` / `actions/download-artifact` to pass the built binaries between jobs.

6. **Version in wrapper package.json is hardcoded to 0.3.0**: build-packages.js updates this at build time, but it should match the current release.

## What I Need

A step-by-step implementation plan covering:
1. Fixing the wrapper package (bin shim)
2. Fixing platform package.json (remove bin entries from build script)  
3. Fixing the release workflow (artifact passing between jobs)
4. Verifying GoReleaser output paths
5. Steps for the user to set up NPM_TOKEN
6. How to do a test publish (maybe a prerelease)
7. Verifying the curl installer works against existing releases
8. Filing a Homebrew devdash issue

Consider that the user is new to npm publishing — include guidance on npm org setup, token creation, etc.

content.text:
Good -- agent_setup.go does not reference the repo URL. Now I have everything I need. Let me write the comprehensive plan.

---

Here is the complete implementation plan.

## Overview of Findings

The NPM distribution scaffolding has three confirmed blocking bugs and requires one-time npm account setup. The curl installer works correctly today. Moving the repo to `@devdashproject` org is a significant cross-cutting concern that touches 40 files (66 occurrences) and the Go module path.

### What Works Today
- GoReleaser builds and publishes GitHub releases successfully (v0.3.0, v0.3.1, v0.3.2 all have correct archives)
- The curl/bash installer (`install.sh`) works correctly -- URL pattern matches actual release asset names, HTTP 302 confirms downloads work
- The bin shim (`npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash`) exists and follows the correct pattern (Node.js launcher that resolves the platform binary via `require.resolve`)
- The `self-update` command correctly handles npm, git, and direct binary installs

### What Is Broken
From the v0.3.2 release workflow logs, I can confirm:
1. **No artifact passing**: The `npm-publish` job has no access to `dist/` from the `release` job. Build-packages.js prints "Warning: binary not found for darwin/arm64" etc. for all platforms.
2. **No NPM_TOKEN**: The wrapper publish fails with `ENEEDAUTH` ("This command requires you to be logged in").
3. **Platform packages never created**: Because binaries were missing, the `continue` in build-packages.js skipped directory creation. The publish loop then hit ENOENT on each platform package.json (swallowed by `|| true`).

---

## Step-by-Step Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Fix the NPM Package Structure (Code Changes)

#### 1A. Remove `bin` entries from platform packages in build-packages.js

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/npm/build-packages.js`

At line 112, the generated platform `package.json` includes `bin: { devdash: "bin/${binaryName}" }`. This is wrong. Esbuild's platform packages (e.g., `@esbuild/darwin-arm64`) have **no** `bin` entries -- they just provide the raw binary. The wrapper package owns the `bin` entry.

**Change**: Remove lines 112-114 (the `bin` property) from the generated package.json object. The platform packages should only have `name`, `version`, `description`, `os`, `cpu`, `repository`, and `license`.

#### 1B. Add Windows entries to the bin shim

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash`

The `PLATFORM_PACKAGES` map (lines 8-13) is missing:
- `"win32-x64": "@devdashproject/devdash-win32-x64"`
- `"win32-arm64": "@devdashproject/devdash-win32-arm64"`

These exist in `postinstall.js` (lines 5-10) but were never added to the bin shim. Add them.

Also, for Windows, the binary name is `devdash.exe`, not `devdash`. The `require.resolve` on line 28 hardcodes `bin/devdash`. It needs to handle Windows:

```javascript
const binaryName = process.platform === "win32" ? "devdash.exe" : "devdash";
return require.resolve(`${pkg}/bin/${binaryName}`);
```

#### 1C. Fix GoReleaser dist path lookup in build-packages.js

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/npm/build-packages.js`

Lines 83-84 construct the path as `devdash_${platform.os}_${platform.goreleaseArch}`, and lines 88-89 try `_v1` as a fallback. In GoReleaser v2, amd64 builds get a `_v1` suffix (the GOAMD64 version). The current code tries the un-suffixed path first, then `_v1`. This works but is fragile.

**Better approach**: Read `dist/artifacts.json` (which GoReleaser always produces) to find the exact path for each binary. This eliminates all guessing. The artifacts.json contains entries with `"type": "Binary"`, `"goos"`, `"goarch"`, and `"path"` fields.

Alternatively, keep the current approach but also try the GoReleaser v2 format `devdash_${platform.os}_${platform.goreleaseArch}_v1` for amd64 only (currently it tries `_v1` for all arches, which is slightly wasteful but harmless).

**Recommendation**: Use `artifacts.json` parsing. It is more robust and future-proof. Here is the logic:

```javascript
const artifactsPath = path.join(distDir, "artifacts.json");
const artifacts = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(artifactsPath, "utf8"));
const binaries = artifacts.filter(a => a.type === "Binary");
// Then for each platform, find the matching binary by goos/goarch
```

### Phase 2: Fix the Release Workflow

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/.github/workflows/release.yml`

#### 2A. Upload GoReleaser dist as artifact

In the `release` job, after the GoReleaser step, add:

```yaml
- name: Upload dist
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: dist
    path: dist/
    retention-days: 1
```

**Important**: This needs to upload the entire `dist/` directory which contains the binary directories (e.g., `dist/devdash_darwin_arm64/devdash`) and `artifacts.json`. The archives (`.tar.gz`, `.zip`) are also there but not needed for npm; however, uploading the whole directory is simplest.

#### 2B. Download artifact in npm-publish job

In the `npm-publish` job, before the "Build npm packages" step, add:

```yaml
- name: Download dist
  uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: dist
    path: dist/
```

#### 2C. Add error handling for platform publishes

Currently the platform publish loop uses `|| true`, which silently swallows all errors including auth failures. Change it so that actual publishing errors (not "already published" 403s) cause the workflow to fail:

```yaml
- name: Publish platform packages
  run: |
    for dir in npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 npm/devdash-darwin-x64 npm/devdash-linux-x64 npm/devdash-linux-arm64 npm/devdash-win32-x64 npm/devdash-win32-arm64; do
      if [ -d "$dir" ]; then
        cd "$dir"
        npm publish --access public
        cd ../..
      else
        echo "ERROR: $dir does not exist"
        exit 1
      fi
    done
  env:
    NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
```

### Phase 3: NPM Account and Org Setup (Manual Steps)

These are manual steps for the user, not code changes.

#### 3A. Create an npm account (if not already done)

1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/signup
2. Create an account (e.g., username `jasonmassey` or `devdashproject`)
3. Verify your email

#### 3B. Create the `@devdashproject` npm organization

1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/org/create
2. Organization name: `devdashproject`
3. Choose the free tier (public packages only, which is what you want)
4. This creates the `@devdashproject` scope

#### 3C. Create an npm automation token

1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/settings/YOUR_USERNAME/tokens
2. Click "Generate New Token"
3. Choose "Automation" type (this bypasses 2FA for CI/CD -- required for GitHub Actions)
4. Copy the token

#### 3D. Add NPM_TOKEN to GitHub repo secrets

1. Go to https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/settings/secrets/actions
2. Click "New repository secret"
3. Name: `NPM_TOKEN`
4. Value: paste the automation token from step 3C

### Phase 4: Test Publish (Prerelease)

#### 4A. Do a local dry-run first

Before pushing a tag, test locally:

1. Run GoReleaser in snapshot mode to get dist/ output:
   ```bash
   goreleaser release --snapshot --clean
   ```
2. Run build-packages.js with a test version:
   ```bash
   cd npm && node build-packages.js 0.4.0-rc.1
   ```
3. Verify the generated packages look correct:
   ```bash
   ls -la npm/devdash-darwin-arm64/bin/
   cat npm/devdash-darwin-arm64/package.json
   cat npm/devdash-cli/package.json
   ```
4. Dry-run publish each package:
   ```bash
   cd npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 && npm publish --access public --dry-run
   cd ../devdash-cli && npm publish --access public --dry-run
   ```

#### 4B. Do a real prerelease via tag

1. Create a prerelease tag:
   ```bash
   git tag v0.4.0-rc.1
   git push origin v0.4.0-rc.1
   ```
2. GoReleaser's `prerelease: auto` setting will mark it as a prerelease on GitHub
3. The npm-publish job will publish `0.4.0-rc.1` versions
4. Verify: `npm view @devdashproject/devdash-cli versions`
5. Test install: `npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.1`

**Note**: npm treats versions with `-rc.X` suffixes as prerelease and will NOT install them with `npm install @devdashproject/devdash-cli` (which gets `latest` tag). Users must explicitly request the version. This is safe.

#### 4C. Add npm dist-tag for prereleases

In the workflow, detect prerelease versions and publish with a `next` tag instead of `latest`:

```yaml
- name: Determine npm tag
  id: npm-tag
  run: |
    VERSION="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"
    if [[ "$VERSION" == *-* ]]; then
      echo "TAG=next" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    else
      echo "TAG=latest" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    fi

- name: Publish wrapper package
  run: |
    cd npm/devdash-cli
    npm publish --access public --tag ${{ steps.npm-tag.outputs.TAG }}
```

### Phase 5: Verify the Curl Installer

The install.sh already works correctly. I verified:
- URL pattern `devdash_${VERSION}_${OS}_${ARCH}.${EXT}` matches actual release asset names
- HTTP 302 redirect confirms the download URL is valid for darwin/arm64
- Archive contents have the binary at the top level (not nested in a subdirectory), matching the `tar -xzf` + `cp "$TMPDIR/$BINARY"` logic in install.sh
- The GitHub API `/releases/latest` endpoint returns the correct tag

**One issue**: if the repo moves to `devdashproject/devdash-cli-go`, the install.sh `REPO` variable on line 8 and the usage comment on line 5 need updating. GitHub does set up redirects for transferred repos, but relying on that permanently is fragile.

### Phase 6: Repository Transfer to @devdashproject GitHub Org

This is the biggest cross-cutting change. Here is what it entails:

#### 6A. Create the GitHub organization

1. Go to https://github.com/organizations/plan
2. Create org named `devdashproject`
3. Choose free tier

#### 6B. Transfer the repository

1. Go to https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/settings (Danger Zone at bottom)
2. Click "Transfer" 
3. Transfer to `devdashproject` org
4. GitHub will set up automatic redirects from the old URL

#### 6C. Update Go module path

This is the most invasive change. The Go module path `github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` is embedded in:
- `go.mod` (line 1)
- 33 Go source files (56 import occurrences)
- `.goreleaser.yml` (ldflags on line 22)

**Option A: Change the module path** (recommended for a clean break)

Change `go.mod` module to `github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli-go`, then update all 33 Go files. This is a mechanical find-and-replace. Use `go mod edit -module` and then a global sed or similar.

**Option B: Keep the old module path with a redirect**

GitHub sets up git redirects for transferred repos, so `go get github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` would still work. But this is confusing for contributors and would rot over time.

**Recommendation**: Option A. Do it as a single commit. The Go toolchain cares about the module path matching the repo URL.

#### 6D. Update all non-Go references

40 files total, but after Go files are done, the remaining files are:
- `install.sh` (lines 5, 7) -- REPO variable and usage comment
- `README.md` (lines 14, 23) -- install URL and releases link

- `.goreleaser.yml` (line 22) -- ldflags module path; also lines 47-48 release owner/name
- `internal/commands/self_update.go` (lines 66, 94) -- hardcoded GitHub API URLs

#### 6E. Update GitHub Actions secrets

After the transfer, repo secrets (`NPM_TOKEN`) stay with the repo. The `GITHUB_TOKEN` auto-generates. No action needed here.

#### 6F. Sequencing decision

**Option 1**: Transfer repo first, then fix NPM.
- Pro: All URLs are correct from the start, npm packages ship with the org URL
- Con: Broken install.sh and self-update until the references are updated

**Option 2**: Fix NPM first, publish under current repo, transfer later.
- Pro: Get npm working quickly, repo transfer is separate
- Con: First npm publish has old URLs, need to republish after transfer

**Recommendation**: Option 1 -- transfer first. The install.sh and self-update will continue working via GitHub's redirect, giving you time to update the references. Do it all in one release cycle.

### Phase 7: Homebrew Tap (Future)

This is noted as "file an issue" rather than implement now. The standard approach for a custom tap:

3. Users install via `brew install devdashproject/tap/devdash`
4. GoReleaser can automate formula updates via its `brews` section

This is independent of the npm work and can be done anytime after the org transfer.

toolUseResult.prompt:
I need a plan to get NPM binary distribution working for a Go CLI tool. The scaffolding exists but has never been published. I also need to verify the curl/bash installer works.

## Current State

**GoReleaser** (`.goreleaser.yml`):
- Builds for 6 platforms: darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, linux/arm64, windows/amd64, windows/arm64
- Archives named: `devdash_<version>_<os>_<arch>.tar.gz` (or .zip for windows)
- Binary name: `devdash`
- Build output: `dist/` directory, build ID is `devdash`
- GoReleaser v2 format

**NPM wrapper** (`npm/devdash-cli/package.json`):
- Scoped to `@devdashproject/devdash-cli`
- Has `"bin": { "devdash": "bin/devdash" }` — but **no actual bin/devdash file exists**
- optionalDependencies list 6 platform packages
- postinstall.js tries to resolve binary from optional dep, falls back to `npm install`

**NPM build script** (`npm/build-packages.js`):
- Reads GoReleaser output from `../dist/`
- Creates 6 platform packages under `npm/devdash-<platform>/`
- Each platform package gets: `package.json` with `os`/`cpu` fields, and `bin/devdash` binary
- Platform packages ALSO have `"bin": { "devdash": "bin/<binary>" }` entries
- Expects binary at `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash` or `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>_v1/devdash`

**Release workflow** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`):
- Job 1: GoReleaser builds + publishes GitHub release
- Job 2 (needs job 1): runs build-packages.js, publishes 6 platform packages + wrapper to npm
- Uses `NPM_TOKEN` secret

**Curl installer** (`install.sh`):
- Fetches latest release from GitHub API
- Downloads archive for detected platform
- Extracts and installs to `~/.local/bin`

## Issues I've Identified

1. **Missing bin shim in wrapper package**: The wrapper's `"bin": { "devdash": "bin/devdash" }` points to a file that doesn't exist. When npm installs, it needs something at that path. The standard pattern (used by esbuild, turbo, SWC) is a JavaScript launcher script that finds the platform-specific binary and spawns it.

2. **Platform packages shouldn't have bin entries**: If both the wrapper AND platform packages define `"bin": { "devdash": ... }`, npm might get confused. The pattern is: wrapper has the bin entry (with JS shim), platform packages just provide the raw binary without bin entries.

3. **GoReleaser output path uncertainty**: build-packages.js expects binaries at `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash`. GoReleaser v2 may use different directory naming. The script has a `_v1` fallback but this may not be correct. Need to verify actual output.

4. **NPM_TOKEN not configured**: The workflow uses `secrets.NPM_TOKEN` but the user has never published, so this likely isn't set up.

5. **Workflow doesn't download GoReleaser artifacts**: The npm-publish job checks out the code but doesn't have access to the `dist/` directory from the GoReleaser job. It needs to use `actions/upload-artifact` / `actions/download-artifact` to pass the built binaries between jobs.

6. **Version in wrapper package.json is hardcoded to 0.3.0**: build-packages.js updates this at build time, but it should match the current release.

## What I Need

A step-by-step implementation plan covering:
1. Fixing the wrapper package (bin shim)
2. Fixing platform package.json (remove bin entries from build script)  
3. Fixing the release workflow (artifact passing between jobs)
4. Verifying GoReleaser output paths
5. Steps for the user to set up NPM_TOKEN
6. How to do a test publish (maybe a prerelease)
7. Verifying the curl installer works against existing releases
8. Filing a Homebrew devdash issue

Consider that the user is new to npm publishing — include guidance on npm org setup, token creation, etc.

Good -- agent_setup.go does not reference the repo URL. Now I have everything I need. Let me write the comprehensive plan.

---

Here is the complete implementation plan.

## Overview of Findings

The NPM distribution scaffolding has three confirmed blocking bugs and requires one-time npm account setup. The curl installer works correctly today. Moving the repo to `@devdashproject` org is a significant cross-cutting concern that touches 40 files (66 occurrences) and the Go module path.

### What Works Today
- GoReleaser builds and publishes GitHub releases successfully (v0.3.0, v0.3.1, v0.3.2 all have correct archives)
- The curl/bash installer (`install.sh`) works correctly -- URL pattern matches actual release asset names, HTTP 302 confirms downloads work
- The bin shim (`npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash`) exists and follows the correct pattern (Node.js launcher that resolves the platform binary via `require.resolve`)
- The `self-update` command correctly handles npm, git, and direct binary installs

### What Is Broken
From the v0.3.2 release workflow logs, I can confirm:
1. **No artifact passing**: The `npm-publish` job has no access to `dist/` from the `release` job. Build-packages.js prints "Warning: binary not found for darwin/arm64" etc. for all platforms.
2. **No NPM_TOKEN**: The wrapper publish fails with `ENEEDAUTH` ("This command requires you to be logged in").
3. **Platform packages never created**: Because binaries were missing, the `continue` in build-packages.js skipped directory creation. The publish loop then hit ENOENT on each platform package.json (swallowed by `|| true`).

---

## Step-by-Step Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Fix the NPM Package Structure (Code Changes)

#### 1A. Remove `bin` entries from platform packages in build-packages.js

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/npm/build-packages.js`

At line 112, the generated platform `package.json` includes `bin: { devdash: "bin/${binaryName}" }`. This is wrong. Esbuild's platform packages (e.g., `@esbuild/darwin-arm64`) have **no** `bin` entries -- they just provide the raw binary. The wrapper package owns the `bin` entry.

**Change**: Remove lines 112-114 (the `bin` property) from the generated package.json object. The platform packages should only have `name`, `version`, `description`, `os`, `cpu`, `repository`, and `license`.

#### 1B. Add Windows entries to the bin shim

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash`

The `PLATFORM_PACKAGES` map (lines 8-13) is missing:
- `"win32-x64": "@devdashproject/devdash-win32-x64"`
- `"win32-arm64": "@devdashproject/devdash-win32-arm64"`

These exist in `postinstall.js` (lines 5-10) but were never added to the bin shim. Add them.

Also, for Windows, the binary name is `devdash.exe`, not `devdash`. The `require.resolve` on line 28 hardcodes `bin/devdash`. It needs to handle Windows:

```javascript
const binaryName = process.platform === "win32" ? "devdash.exe" : "devdash";
return require.resolve(`${pkg}/bin/${binaryName}`);
```

#### 1C. Fix GoReleaser dist path lookup in build-packages.js

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/npm/build-packages.js`

Lines 83-84 construct the path as `devdash_${platform.os}_${platform.goreleaseArch}`, and lines 88-89 try `_v1` as a fallback. In GoReleaser v2, amd64 builds get a `_v1` suffix (the GOAMD64 version). The current code tries the un-suffixed path first, then `_v1`. This works but is fragile.

**Better approach**: Read `dist/artifacts.json` (which GoReleaser always produces) to find the exact path for each binary. This eliminates all guessing. The artifacts.json contains entries with `"type": "Binary"`, `"goos"`, `"goarch"`, and `"path"` fields.

Alternatively, keep the current approach but also try the GoReleaser v2 format `devdash_${platform.os}_${platform.goreleaseArch}_v1` for amd64 only (currently it tries `_v1` for all arches, which is slightly wasteful but harmless).

**Recommendation**: Use `artifacts.json` parsing. It is more robust and future-proof. Here is the logic:

```javascript
const artifactsPath = path.join(distDir, "artifacts.json");
const artifacts = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(artifactsPath, "utf8"));
const binaries = artifacts.filter(a => a.type === "Binary");
// Then for each platform, find the matching binary by goos/goarch
```

### Phase 2: Fix the Release Workflow

**File**: `/Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/.claude/worktrees/rationalize-agent-instructions/.github/workflows/release.yml`

#### 2A. Upload GoReleaser dist as artifact

In the `release` job, after the GoReleaser step, add:

```yaml
- name: Upload dist
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: dist
    path: dist/
    retention-days: 1
```

**Important**: This needs to upload the entire `dist/` directory which contains the binary directories (e.g., `dist/devdash_darwin_arm64/devdash`) and `artifacts.json`. The archives (`.tar.gz`, `.zip`) are also there but not needed for npm; however, uploading the whole directory is simplest.

#### 2B. Download artifact in npm-publish job

In the `npm-publish` job, before the "Build npm packages" step, add:

```yaml
- name: Download dist
  uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: dist
    path: dist/
```

#### 2C. Add error handling for platform publishes

Currently the platform publish loop uses `|| true`, which silently swallows all errors including auth failures. Change it so that actual publishing errors (not "already published" 403s) cause the workflow to fail:

```yaml
- name: Publish platform packages
  run: |
    for dir in npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 npm/devdash-darwin-x64 npm/devdash-linux-x64 npm/devdash-linux-arm64 npm/devdash-win32-x64 npm/devdash-win32-arm64; do
      if [ -d "$dir" ]; then
        cd "$dir"
        npm publish --access public
        cd ../..
      else
        echo "ERROR: $dir does not exist"
        exit 1
      fi
    done
  env:
    NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
```

### Phase 3: NPM Account and Org Setup (Manual Steps)

These are manual steps for the user, not code changes.

#### 3A. Create an npm account (if not already done)

1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/signup
2. Create an account (e.g., username `jasonmassey` or `devdashproject`)
3. Verify your email

#### 3B. Create the `@devdashproject` npm organization

1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/org/create
2. Organization name: `devdashproject`
3. Choose the free tier (public packages only, which is what you want)
4. This creates the `@devdashproject` scope

#### 3C. Create an npm automation token

1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/settings/YOUR_USERNAME/tokens
2. Click "Generate New Token"
3. Choose "Automation" type (this bypasses 2FA for CI/CD -- required for GitHub Actions)
4. Copy the token

#### 3D. Add NPM_TOKEN to GitHub repo secrets

1. Go to https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/settings/secrets/actions
2. Click "New repository secret"
3. Name: `NPM_TOKEN`
4. Value: paste the automation token from step 3C

### Phase 4: Test Publish (Prerelease)

#### 4A. Do a local dry-run first

Before pushing a tag, test locally:

1. Run GoReleaser in snapshot mode to get dist/ output:
   ```bash
   goreleaser release --snapshot --clean
   ```
2. Run build-packages.js with a test version:
   ```bash
   cd npm && node build-packages.js 0.4.0-rc.1
   ```
3. Verify the generated packages look correct:
   ```bash
   ls -la npm/devdash-darwin-arm64/bin/
   cat npm/devdash-darwin-arm64/package.json
   cat npm/devdash-cli/package.json
   ```
4. Dry-run publish each package:
   ```bash
   cd npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 && npm publish --access public --dry-run
   cd ../devdash-cli && npm publish --access public --dry-run
   ```

#### 4B. Do a real prerelease via tag

1. Create a prerelease tag:
   ```bash
   git tag v0.4.0-rc.1
   git push origin v0.4.0-rc.1
   ```
2. GoReleaser's `prerelease: auto` setting will mark it as a prerelease on GitHub
3. The npm-publish job will publish `0.4.0-rc.1` versions
4. Verify: `npm view @devdashproject/devdash-cli versions`
5. Test install: `npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.1`

**Note**: npm treats versions with `-rc.X` suffixes as prerelease and will NOT install them with `npm install @devdashproject/devdash-cli` (which gets `latest` tag). Users must explicitly request the version. This is safe.

#### 4C. Add npm dist-tag for prereleases

In the workflow, detect prerelease versions and publish with a `next` tag instead of `latest`:

```yaml
- name: Determine npm tag
  id: npm-tag
  run: |
    VERSION="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"
    if [[ "$VERSION" == *-* ]]; then
      echo "TAG=next" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    else
      echo "TAG=latest" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    fi

- name: Publish wrapper package
  run: |
    cd npm/devdash-cli
    npm publish --access public --tag ${{ steps.npm-tag.outputs.TAG }}
```

### Phase 5: Verify the Curl Installer

The install.sh already works correctly. I verified:
- URL pattern `devdash_${VERSION}_${OS}_${ARCH}.${EXT}` matches actual release asset names
- HTTP 302 redirect confirms the download URL is valid for darwin/arm64
- Archive contents have the binary at the top level (not nested in a subdirectory), matching the `tar -xzf` + `cp "$TMPDIR/$BINARY"` logic in install.sh
- The GitHub API `/releases/latest` endpoint returns the correct tag

**One issue**: if the repo moves to `devdashproject/devdash-cli-go`, the install.sh `REPO` variable on line 8 and the usage comment on line 5 need updating. GitHub does set up redirects for transferred repos, but relying on that permanently is fragile.

### Phase 6: Repository Transfer to @devdashproject GitHub Org

This is the biggest cross-cutting change. Here is what it entails:

#### 6A. Create the GitHub organization

1. Go to https://github.com/organizations/plan
2. Create org named `devdashproject`
3. Choose free tier

#### 6B. Transfer the repository

1. Go to https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/settings (Danger Zone at bottom)
2. Click "Transfer" 
3. Transfer to `devdashproject` org
4. GitHub will set up automatic redirects from the old URL

#### 6C. Update Go module path

This is the most invasive change. The Go module path `github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` is embedded in:
- `go.mod` (line 1)
- 33 Go source files (56 import occurrences)
- `.goreleaser.yml` (ldflags on line 22)

**Option A: Change the module path** (recommended for a clean break)

Change `go.mod` module to `github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli-go`, then update all 33 Go files. This is a mechanical find-and-replace. Use `go mod edit -module` and then a global sed or similar.

**Option B: Keep the old module path with a redirect**

GitHub sets up git redirects for transferred repos, so `go get github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` would still work. But this is confusing for contributors and would rot over time.

**Recommendation**: Option A. Do it as a single commit. The Go toolchain cares about the module path matching the repo URL.

#### 6D. Update all non-Go references

40 files total, but after Go files are done, the remaining files are:
- `install.sh` (lines 5, 7) -- REPO variable and usage comment
- `README.md` (lines 14, 23) -- install URL and releases link
- `GETTING-STARTED.md` (line 19) -- install URL
- `npm/devdash-cli/package.json` (line 29) -- repository URL
- `npm/build-packages.js` (line 117) -- repository URL in generated package.json
- `.goreleaser.yml` (line 22) -- ldflags module path; also lines 47-48 release owner/name
- `internal/commands/self_update.go` (lines 66, 94) -- hardcoded GitHub API URLs

#### 6E. Update GitHub Actions secrets

After the transfer, repo secrets (`NPM_TOKEN`) stay with the repo. The `GITHUB_TOKEN` auto-generates. No action needed here.

#### 6F. Sequencing decision

**Option 1**: Transfer repo first, then fix NPM.
- Pro: All URLs are correct from the start, npm packages ship with the org URL
- Con: Broken install.sh and self-update until the references are updated

**Option 2**: Fix NPM first, publish under current repo, transfer later.
- Pro: Get npm working quickly, repo transfer is separate
- Con: First npm publish has old URLs, need to republish after transfer

**Recommendation**: Option 1 -- transfer first. The install.sh and self-update will continue working via GitHub's redirect, giving you time to update the references. Do it all in one release cycle.

### Phase 7: Homebrew Tap (Future)

This is noted as "file an issue" rather than implement now. The standard approach for a custom tap:

1. Create repo `devdashproject/homebrew-tap`
2. Add a formula that downloads the GitHub release archive
3. Users install via `brew install devdashproject/tap/devdash`
4. GoReleaser can automate formula updates via its `brews` section

This is independent of the npm work and can be done anytime after the org transfer.

Confirmed — both bugs:
1. **No binaries found** — `dist/` doesn't exist in the npm-publish job (no artifact passing)
2. **ENEEDAUTH** — no NPM_TOKEN configured
3. Platform packages for Windows weren't even attempted (the `continue` skipped them since binaries were missing)
4. The platform publish step "succeeded" (green check) because each `|| true` swallowed the missing directory errors

Now I have everything I need. Let me write the plan.

input.content:
# NPM Distribution & Install Verification

## Context

The devdash CLI has npm publishing scaffolding that has never successfully published. All three release workflow runs (v0.3.0, v0.3.1, v0.3.2) failed at the npm-publish stage with two bugs: (1) no artifact passing between jobs — `dist/` doesn't exist so all binaries are "not found", (2) no NPM_TOKEN configured — wrapper publish fails with ENEEDAUTH. The curl installer and GitHub releases work fine.

## Bugs confirmed from v0.3.2 release logs

- `Warning: binary not found for darwin/arm64` (repeated for all platforms)
- Platform packages never created → publish loop silently skipped via `|| true`
- Wrapper publish hit `ENEEDAUTH`

## Plan

### Step 1: Fix release workflow — artifact passing

**File**: `.github/workflows/release.yml`

The `release` job builds into `dist/` but the `npm-publish` job has no access to it. Add `actions/upload-artifact` after GoReleaser and `actions/download-artifact` before build-packages.

### Step 2: Fix build-packages.js — use artifacts.json for path lookup

**File**: `npm/build-packages.js`

Currently guesses paths like `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash` with a `_v1` fallback. GoReleaser v2 produces `dist/artifacts.json` with exact binary paths. Parse that instead of guessing.

Also remove the `bin` entry from generated platform `package.json` — only the wrapper should have a `bin` entry (the JS shim). Platform packages just provide the raw binary.

### Step 3: Fix bin shim — add Windows support

**File**: `npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash`

Missing `win32-x64` and `win32-arm64` entries in PLATFORM_PACKAGES map (lines 7-12). Also needs to handle `devdash.exe` binary name on Windows:
```js
const binaryName = process.platform === "win32" ? "devdash.exe" : "devdash";
```

### Step 4: Fix release workflow — prerelease npm tags & error handling

**File**: `.github/workflows/release.yml`

- Add dist-tag detection: prerelease versions (`-rc.X`, `-beta.X`) publish with `--tag next`, stable with `--tag latest`
- Remove `|| true` from platform publish loop — replace with proper error handling so auth/build failures aren't swallowed

### Step 5: Manual — npm account setup (user does this)

1. Verify `@devdashproject` org exists on npmjs.com (or create at https://www.npmjs.com/org/create)
2. Create automation token at https://www.npmjs.com/settings/tokens — choose "Automation" type (bypasses 2FA for CI)
3. Add `NPM_TOKEN` secret at https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/settings/secrets/actions

### Step 6: Local dry-run test

1. `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean` — produces `dist/` locally
2. `cd npm && node build-packages.js 0.4.0-test` — verify packages generated
3. Inspect `npm/devdash-darwin-arm64/package.json` — no `bin` entry, correct `os`/`cpu`
4. Inspect `npm/devdash-cli/package.json` — version updated, deps updated
5. `npm publish --dry-run` on wrapper and one platform package

### Step 7: Prerelease tag test

1. Tag `v0.4.0-rc.1` and push
2. Monitor GitHub Actions
3. Verify: `npm view @devdashproject/devdash-cli versions`
4. Test: `npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.1`
5. Run `devdash version` to confirm

### Step 8: Verify curl installer

The installer works today (confirmed: URL patterns match release assets, HTTP 302 works). Just run:
```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
```

### Step 9: File Homebrew issue

Create devdash issue for Homebrew tap setup (separate repo `devdashproject/homebrew-tap`, GoReleaser `brews` config).

## Critical files

- `.github/workflows/release.yml` — artifact passing + npm tag logic
- `npm/build-packages.js` — artifacts.json parsing, remove platform bin entries
- `npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash` — Windows support in shim
- `npm/devdash-cli/postinstall.js` — already correct (has Windows entries)
- `.goreleaser.yml` — no changes needed

## Verification

1. `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean` locally
2. `node npm/build-packages.js 0.0.0-test` — all 6 platforms built
3. `npm publish --dry-run` on wrapper + one platform
4. Push `v0.4.0-rc.1` tag → all CI green → `npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.1` works
5. `curl ... | sh` installs correct version

lines: -# Update `help cli` and `prime` for tier-aware documentation

-The `help cli` topic is significantly out of date — it documents ~20 of ~40+ commands. Meanwhile, execution/automation commands (dispatch, jobs, analyze, diagnose, score, reconcile-tasks) are going behind a paywall, and sync/import are being reworked as provider-based integrations. The `prime` command (agent context injection) should not teach agents about commands they can't use on the free tier.

+The devdash CLI has npm publishing scaffolding that has never successfully published. All three release workflow runs (v0.3.0, v0.3.1, v0.3.2) failed at the npm-publish stage with two bugs: (1) no artifact passing between jobs — `dist/` doesn't exist so all binaries are "not found", (2) no NPM_TOKEN configured — wrapper publish fails with ENEEDAUTH. The curl installer and GitHub releases work fine.
-This continues the pattern we established earlier this session by pulling `report` out of the default workflow.
+## Bugs confirmed from v0.3.2 release logs
+- `Warning: binary not found for darwin/arm64` (repeated for all platforms)
+- Platform packages never created → publish loop silently skipped via `|| true`
-Replace the flat "Commands" section (lines 36-95) with tiered sections:
+### Step 1: Fix release workflow — artifact passing
-- **Issue Tracking (Core)** — existing commands, unchanged text (preserves test assertions for `ready` and `show` descriptions)
-No admin section — `admin` commands will be removed from the user-facing CLI entirely (separate admin CLI TBD).
+The `release` job builds into `dist/` but the `npm-publish` job has no access to it. Add `actions/upload-artifact` after GoReleaser and `actions/download-artifact` before build-packages.
-ID Formats, --since Format, Priority, and Examples sections stay unchanged.
+### Step 2: Fix build-packages.js — use artifacts.json for path lookup
-Line 141: Remove `jobs show`, `score <id>`, and `sync` from the "commands that return JSON" list. These are paid-tier or WIP. Keep `show`, `find`, `activity`, `comments`, `token list`.
+Currently guesses paths like `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash` with a `_v1` fallback. GoReleaser v2 produces `dist/artifacts.json` with exact binary paths. Parse that instead of guessing.
-No other prime.go changes needed — Quick Reference and On-Demand Reference are already clean from the earlier `report` removal.
+Also remove the `bin` entry from generated platform `package.json` — only the wrapper should have a `bin` entry (the JS shim). Platform packages just provide the raw binary.
+### Step 3: Fix bin shim — add Windows support
-Already clean. No references to paid-tier or WIP commands.
+Missing `win32-x64` and `win32-arm64` entries in PLATFORM_PACKAGES map (lines 7-12). Also needs to handle `devdash.exe` binary name on Windows:
-- `TestHelpTopicCLI` (line 561): asserts `"CLI Reference"` — preserved in new header
-- Test at line 667: asserts `"choose what to work on next"` — preserved verbatim in `ready` description
-- Test at line 670: asserts `"Start here when the user already named the issue"` — preserved verbatim in `show` description
+### Step 4: Fix release workflow — prerelease npm tags & error handling
+- Add dist-tag detection: prerelease versions (`-rc.X`, `-beta.X`) publish with `--tag next`, stable with `--tag latest`
+- Remove `|| true` from platform publish loop — replace with proper error handling so auth/build failures aren't swallowed
+### Step 5: Manual — npm account setup (user does this)

# Update `help cli` and `prime` for tier-aware documentation

## Context

The `help cli` topic is significantly out of date — it documents ~20 of ~40+ commands. Meanwhile, execution/automation commands (dispatch, jobs, analyze, diagnose, score, reconcile-tasks) are going behind a paywall, and sync/import are being reworked as provider-based integrations. The `prime` command (agent context injection) should not teach agents about commands they can't use on the free tier.

This continues the pattern we established earlier this session by pulling `report` out of the default workflow.

## Changes

### 1. `internal/commands/help_topics.go` — Reorganize `"cli"` topic

Replace the flat "Commands" section (lines 36-95) with tiered sections:

- **Issue Tracking (Core)** — existing commands, unchanged text (preserves test assertions for `ready` and `show` descriptions)
- **Setup & Utility** — `login`, `init`, `doctor`, `version`, `self-update`, `uninstall`, `agent-setup`, `alias-setup`
- **Project Management** — `project create/list/delete`
- **Token Management** — `token create/list/revoke`
- **Execution & Automation (paid plan)** — `dispatch`, `jobs` (list/show/log/failures), `analyze`, `diagnose`, `score`, `reconcile-tasks`
- **Integration (WIP — being reworked)** — `sync`, `import`

No admin section — `admin` commands will be removed from the user-facing CLI entirely (separate admin CLI TBD).

ID Formats, --since Format, Priority, and Examples sections stay unchanged.

### 2. `internal/commands/prime.go` — Remove paid/WIP from JSON list

Line 141: Remove `jobs show`, `score <id>`, and `sync` from the "commands that return JSON" list. These are paid-tier or WIP. Keep `show`, `find`, `activity`, `comments`, `token list`.

No other prime.go changes needed — Quick Reference and On-Demand Reference are already clean from the earlier `report` removal.

### 3. `CLAUDE.md` — No changes

Already clean. No references to paid-tier or WIP commands.

## Test impact

- `TestHelpTopicCLI` (line 561): asserts `"CLI Reference"` — preserved in new header
- Test at line 667: asserts `"choose what to work on next"` — preserved verbatim in `ready` description
- Test at line 670: asserts `"Start here when the user already named the issue"` — preserved verbatim in `show` description

## Verification

1. `go build ./cmd/devdash` — compiles
2. `go test ./internal/commands/...` — all tests pass
3. `./devdash help cli` — visually confirm tiered sections
4. `./devdash prime` — confirm no paid/WIP commands in output

input.plan:
# NPM Distribution & Install Verification

## Context

The devdash CLI has npm publishing scaffolding that has never successfully published. All three release workflow runs (v0.3.0, v0.3.1, v0.3.2) failed at the npm-publish stage with two bugs: (1) no artifact passing between jobs — `dist/` doesn't exist so all binaries are "not found", (2) no NPM_TOKEN configured — wrapper publish fails with ENEEDAUTH. The curl installer and GitHub releases work fine.

## Bugs confirmed from v0.3.2 release logs

- `Warning: binary not found for darwin/arm64` (repeated for all platforms)
- Platform packages never created → publish loop silently skipped via `|| true`
- Wrapper publish hit `ENEEDAUTH`

## Plan

### Step 1: Fix release workflow — artifact passing

**File**: `.github/workflows/release.yml`

The `release` job builds into `dist/` but the `npm-publish` job has no access to it. Add `actions/upload-artifact` after GoReleaser and `actions/download-artifact` before build-packages.

### Step 2: Fix build-packages.js — use artifacts.json for path lookup

**File**: `npm/build-packages.js`

Currently guesses paths like `dist/devdash_<os>_<arch>/devdash` with a `_v1` fallback. GoReleaser v2 produces `dist/artifacts.json` with exact binary paths. Parse that instead of guessing.

Also remove the `bin` entry from generated platform `package.json` — only the wrapper should have a `bin` entry (the JS shim). Platform packages just provide the raw binary.

### Step 3: Fix bin shim — add Windows support

**File**: `npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash`

Missing `win32-x64` and `win32-arm64` entries in PLATFORM_PACKAGES map (lines 7-12). Also needs to handle `devdash.exe` binary name on Windows:
```js
const binaryName = process.platform === "win32" ? "devdash.exe" : "devdash";
```

### Step 4: Fix release workflow — prerelease npm tags & error handling

**File**: `.github/workflows/release.yml`

- Add dist-tag detection: prerelease versions (`-rc.X`, `-beta.X`) publish with `--tag next`, stable with `--tag latest`
- Remove `|| true` from platform publish loop — replace with proper error handling so auth/build failures aren't swallowed

### Step 5: Manual — npm account setup (user does this)

1. Verify `@devdashproject` org exists on npmjs.com (or create at https://www.npmjs.com/org/create)
2. Create automation token at https://www.npmjs.com/settings/tokens — choose "Automation" type (bypasses 2FA for CI)
3. Add `NPM_TOKEN` secret at https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/settings/secrets/actions

### Step 6: Local dry-run test

1. `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean` — produces `dist/` locally
2. `cd npm && node build-packages.js 0.4.0-test` — verify packages generated
3. Inspect `npm/devdash-darwin-arm64/package.json` — no `bin` entry, correct `os`/`cpu`
4. Inspect `npm/devdash-cli/package.json` — version updated, deps updated
5. `npm publish --dry-run` on wrapper and one platform package

### Step 7: Prerelease tag test

1. Tag `v0.4.0-rc.1` and push
2. Monitor GitHub Actions
3. Verify: `npm view @devdashproject/devdash-cli versions`
4. Test: `npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.1`
5. Run `devdash version` to confirm

### Step 8: Verify curl installer

The installer works today (confirmed: URL patterns match release assets, HTTP 302 works). Just run:
```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
```

### Step 9: File Homebrew issue

Create devdash issue for Homebrew tap setup (separate repo `devdashproject/homebrew-tap`, GoReleaser `brews` config).

## Critical files

- `.github/workflows/release.yml` — artifact passing + npm tag logic
- `npm/build-packages.js` — artifacts.json parsing, remove platform bin entries
- `npm/devdash-cli/bin/devdash` — Windows support in shim
- `npm/devdash-cli/postinstall.js` — already correct (has Windows entries)
- `.goreleaser.yml` — no changes needed

## Verification

1. `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean` locally
2. `node npm/build-packages.js 0.0.0-test` — all 6 platforms built
3. `npm publish --dry-run` on wrapper + one platform
4. Push `v0.4.0-rc.1` tag → all CI green → `npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.1` works
5. `curl ... | sh` installs correct version

content.text:
Got it — create org, rename to `devdash-cli`. What's your thinking on the sequencing question? The tradeoff is:

- **Transfer first**: Everything ships with the right org URLs from day one. But the Go module path change (`github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` → `github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli`) touches ~33 Go files and all docs. This is mechanical but it's a big diff.

- **Fix npm first**: Get publishing working faster under the current repo, but the first npm packages ship with `jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` URLs. You'd need to republish after the transfer.

Or is there a third option you're considering — like creating a fresh repo under the new org rather than transferring?

message.content: help me understand the implications of republishing under a different URL

content.text:
The npm packages contain the repo URL in two places:

1. **`package.json` `repository` field** — metadata only. npm shows it on the package page. Changing it in a later version is trivial and has zero functional impact.

2. **The actual binary inside the package** — the Go binary has the module path baked into it via `ldflags` (`-X ...commands.Version`), but the repo URL itself isn't embedded in the binary. Users don't see it.

So "republishing under a different URL" really means:

- You publish `@devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0` with `repository: github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go`
- Later you publish `@devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.5.0` with `repository: github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli`
- Users see the corrected link on npmjs.com. That's it. No functional difference.

**What actually matters is the Go module path.** If you transfer the repo, Go's module system needs the module path to match the repo URL. GitHub sets up git redirects for transferred repos, so `go get github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go` would still work. But:

- New contributors would see a mismatched module path (confusing)
- Go proxy caches the old path indefinitely — it works but looks wrong
- Best practice is to update `go.mod` and all imports after transfer

**The real question is**: do you want one big release that does org transfer + module rename + npm fix all at once, or break it into smaller steps where the intermediate state is slightly messy but each step is lower risk?

Given nobody is consuming this as a Go library (it's a CLI binary), the module path is purely internal. The cost of shipping one version with the old repo URL in npm metadata is essentially zero.

message.content: I'd prefer to keep the metadata clean from the start; let's work through this big release step by step. Stop and tell me when you need me to take action in GitHub or other third party surfaces

file.content:
version: 2

before:
  hooks:
    - go mod tidy

builds:
  - id: devdash
    main: ./cmd/devdash
    binary: devdash
    env:
      - CGO_ENABLED=0
    goos:
      - darwin
      - linux
      - windows
    goarch:
      - amd64
      - arm64
    ldflags:
      - -s -w
      - -X github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/internal/commands.Version={{.Version}}

archives:
  - id: default
    format: tar.gz
    format_overrides:
      - goos: windows
        format: zip
    name_template: "devdash_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}"
    files:
      - LICENSE

checksum:
  name_template: "checksums.txt"

changelog:
  sort: asc
  filters:
    exclude:
      - "^docs:"
      - "^test:"
      - "^ci:"

release:
  github:
    owner: jasonmassey
    name: devdash-cli-go
  draft: false
  prerelease: auto

file.content:
#!/bin/sh
set -e

# DevDash CLI installer
# Usage: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh

REPO="jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go"
INSTALL_DIR="${DEVDASH_INSTALL_DIR:-$HOME/.local/bin}"
BINARY="devdash"

# Detect platform
OS="$(uname -s | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')"
ARCH="$(uname -m)"

case "$ARCH" in
  x86_64|amd64) ARCH="amd64" ;;
  arm64|aarch64) ARCH="arm64" ;;
  *)
    echo "Error: unsupported architecture: $ARCH"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

case "$OS" in
  darwin|linux) EXT="tar.gz" ;;
  mingw*|msys*|cygwin*|windows*) OS="windows"; EXT="zip" ;;
  *)
    echo "Error: unsupported OS: $OS"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

# Get latest version
echo "Fetching latest version..."
VERSION=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/latest" | grep '"tag_name"' | sed -E 's/.*"v([^"]+)".*/\1/')

if [ -z "$VERSION" ]; then
  echo "Error: could not determine latest version"
  exit 1
fi

echo "Installing devdash v${VERSION} (${OS}/${ARCH})..."

# Download
ARCHIVE="devdash_${VERSION}_${OS}_${ARCH}.${EXT}"
URL="https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/v${VERSION}/${ARCHIVE}"

if [ "$OS" = "windows" ]; then
  BINARY="devdash.exe"
fi

TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap 'rm -rf "$TMPDIR"' EXIT

echo "Downloading $URL..."
curl -fsSL "$URL" -o "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE"

# Extract
if [ "$EXT" = "zip" ]; then
  unzip -q "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE" -d "$TMPDIR"
else
  tar -xzf "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE" -C "$TMPDIR"
fi

# Install
mkdir -p "$INSTALL_DIR"
cp "$TMPDIR/$BINARY" "$INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"
chmod +x "$INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"

echo "Installed to $INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"

# Check PATH
case ":$PATH:" in
  *":$INSTALL_DIR:"*) ;;
  *)
    echo ""
    echo "Note: $INSTALL_DIR is not in your PATH."
    echo "Add it with:"
    echo "  export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\""
    echo ""
    SHELL_NAME=$(basename "$SHELL")
    case "$SHELL_NAME" in
      zsh)  echo "  echo 'export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\"' >> ~/.zshrc" ;;
      bash) echo "  echo 'export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\"' >> ~/.bashrc" ;;
      fish) echo "  fish_add_path $INSTALL_DIR" ;;
    esac
    ;;
esac

echo ""
echo "Run 'devdash version' to verify."

37	    "developer-tools"
38	  ]
39	}
40

1	# devdash-cli-go
2	
3	Dev-Dash CLI — AI-powered task tracking for coding agents.
4	
5	A fast, single-binary CLI that wraps the [Dev-Dash](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app/) REST API for managing tasks, dependencies, and agent jobs from the terminal. Built in Go with zero runtime dependencies.
6	
7	This CLI can be run in your terminal but is optimized for agents. Don't feel like you need to memorize the commands; your agent should be familiar enough!
8	
9	**New here?** Check out the **[Getting Started guide](GETTING-STARTED.md)** for a friendly walkthrough, or read **[Understanding the Workflow](UNDERSTANDING-THE-WORKFLOW.md)** for the philosophy behind Dev-Dash.
10	
11	## Install
12	
13	```bash
14	curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
15	```
16	
17	Or via npm:
18	
19	```bash
20	npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
21	```
22	
23	Or download a binary directly from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/releases).
24	
25	### Build from source
26	
27	```bash
28	go build -o devdash ./cmd/devdash
29	```
30	
31	## Setup
32	
33	```bash
34	# 1. Authenticate (opens browser for Google OAuth)
35	devdash login
36	
37	# 2. Link to a project
38	devdash init                    # Auto-detects from git remote
39	devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
40	devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix
41	
42	# 3. Verify
43	devdash doctor
44	```
45	
46	During init you'll be offered to set up the `dd` alias and configure AI agent instructions.
47	
48	## Quick Start
49	
50	```bash
51	devdash ready                              # Show tasks ready to work
52	devdash show <id>                          # View task details
53	devdash update <id> --status=in_progress   # Claim work
54	# ... do the work ...
55	devdash close <id>                         # Mark complete
56	```
57	
58	### Creating tasks
59	
60	```bash
61	devdash create --title="Fix login bug" --type=bug --priority=1
62	devdash create --title="Add dark mode" --type=feature --description="Support system theme"
63	```
64	
65	### Managing dependencies
66	
67	```bash
68	devdash create --title="Write tests" --type=task
69	devdash dep add <test-id> <feature-id>   # Tests depend on feature
70	devdash blocked                           # See what's waiting
71	```
72	
73	## All Commands
74	
75	| Command | Description |
76	|---------|-------------|
77	| `login` | Authenticate via browser (tries ports 18787-18792) |
78	| `init [name-or-id]` | Link repo to a project (auto-detect, name, ID prefix, or interactive picker) |
79	| `project create` | Create a new project (`--name`, `--repo`, `--description`) |
80	| `project list` | List all your projects |
81	| `project delete <id>` | Delete a project (`--force` to skip confirmation) |
82	| `list [--status=X] [--since=X]` | List tasks (optional status and time filter) |
83	| `ready [--since=X]` | Tasks with no blockers, sorted by automability then priority |
84	| `blocked` | Tasks waiting on dependencies |
85	| `stale [--since=X]` | In-progress tasks with no recent activity |
86	| `show <id>` | Task details with dependencies |
87	| `create --title="..."` | Create task (`--type`, `--priority`, `--description`, `--parent`) |
88	| `update <id> --key=val` | Update fields (`--status`, `--priority`, `--owner`, `--title`, `--description`, `--pre-instructions`) |
89	| `close <id> [...] [--pr=URL] [--commit=SHA] [--summary="..."]` | Close one or more tasks (with optional completion metadata) |
90	| `delete <id> [--force] [--cascade]` | Delete a task (`--cascade` deletes children) |
91	| `dep add <id> <dep-id>` | Add dependency (id depends on dep-id) |
92	| `dep remove <id> <dep-id>` | Remove dependency |
93	| `comment <id> --body="..."` | Add a comment to a task |
94	| `comments <id>` | List comments on a task |
95	| `activity [--since=X]` | View activity log |
96	| `report <id> --status=X` | Log agent progress (`code_complete\|committed\|pushed\|error`) |
97	| `find <uuid>` | Cross-project task lookup |
98	| `diagnose <id>` | Investigate task: status, job history, failure details |
99	| `jobs [--bead=<id>]` | List recent jobs (optionally filtered by task) |
100	| `reconcile` | AI-powered backlog audit |
101	| `score <id>` | Score task automability |
102	| `analyze <id>` | Analyze a task |
103	| `dispatch <id>` | Dispatch work to an agent |
104	| `sync` | Trigger full reconcile with GitHub |
105	| `import <num> \| --all` | Import GitHub issue(s) into the project |
106	| `stats` | Project statistics |
107	| `prime` | Output workflow context for AI agents (includes project health) |
108	| `doctor` | Check prerequisites and configuration |
109	| `agent-setup` | Configure AI agent instructions (`--agent=X`, `--all`, `--force`) |
110	| `alias-setup` | Add `dd` shortcut alias |
111	| `team` | List project team members |
112	| `token create\|list\|revoke` | Manage API tokens |
113	| `self-update` | Update to latest version |
114	| `uninstall` | Remove the CLI |
115	
116	### Bead IDs
117	
118	Commands accept UUIDs, short prefixes (e.g., `27bf`), or local IDs. Ambiguous prefixes are rejected with suggestions.
119	
120	### dd alias
121	
122	During `devdash init`, you'll be prompted to alias `dd` to `devdash` for shorter commands. This shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (Unix disk copy) — only enable if you don't use that tool.
123	
124	```bash
125	devdash alias-setup
126	```
127	
128	### Agent integration
129	
130	`devdash init` detects AI agent configs (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/, AGENTS.md, etc.) and offers to run `agent-setup` automatically. You can also run it directly:
131	
132	```bash
133	devdash agent-setup              # Interactive — detects your agents
134	devdash agent-setup --agent=claude,codex
135	devdash agent-setup --all --force
136	```
137	
138	The `prime` command outputs structured context for AI coding agents:
139	
140	```bash
141	devdash prime
142	```
143	
144	It includes project name, health stats, command reference, and workflow patterns. Use it as a session hook to inject workflow context automatically.
145	
146	### Multi-project setup
147	
148	Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file linking it to a project. The CLI reads this file to determine which project to use:
149	
150	```bash
151	cd my-project && devdash init    # Links this repo
152	cd other-repo && devdash init    # Links that repo separately
153	```
154	
155	You can also set `DD_PROJECT_ID` and `DD_API_URL` environment variables to override, or use the `--project` flag on any command.
156	
157	## Troubleshooting
158	
159	| Issue | Fix |
160	|-------|-----|
161	| `Not logged in` | Run `devdash login` |
162	| `No project configured` | Run `devdash init` in your repo |
163	| `Port 18787 in use` during login | CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792 |
164	| `API error (401)` | Token expired — re-run `devdash login` |
165	| Commands seem slow | API latency — ensure good network connection |
166	
167	Run `devdash doctor` to check all prerequisites and configuration.
168	
169	## Exit Codes
170	
171	| Code | Meaning |
172	|------|---------|
173	| 0 | Success |
174	| 1 | User error (bad arguments, unknown command) |
175	| 2 | API error (network, auth, server error) |
176	| 3 | Config error (not logged in, no project) |
177	
178	## Platforms
179	
180	Pre-built binaries are available for:
181	
182	- macOS (Apple Silicon and Intel)
183	- Linux (amd64 and arm64)
184	- Windows (amd64 and arm64)
185	
186	## License
187	
188	MIT
