# devdash-cli-go

Dev-Dash CLI — AI-powered task tracking for coding agents.

A fast, single-binary CLI that wraps the [Dev-Dash](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app/) REST API for managing tasks, dependencies, and agent jobs from the terminal. Built in Go with zero runtime dependencies.

This CLI can be run in your terminal but is optimized for agents. Don't feel like you need to memorize the commands; your agent should be familiar enough!

**New here?** Check out the **[Getting Started guide](GETTING-STARTED.md)** for a friendly walkthrough, or read **[Understanding the Workflow](UNDERSTANDING-THE-WORKFLOW.md)** for the philosophy behind Dev-Dash.

## Install

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
```

Or via npm:

```bash
npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
```

Or download a binary directly from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/releases).

### Build from source

```bash
go build -o devdash ./cmd/devdash
```

## Setup

```bash
# 1. Authenticate (opens browser for Google OAuth)
devdash login

# 2. Link to a project
devdash init                    # Auto-detects from git remote
devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix

# 3. Verify
devdash doctor
```

During init you'll be offered to set up the `dd` alias and configure AI agent instructions.

## Quick Start

```bash
devdash ready                              # Show tasks ready to work
devdash show <id>                          # View task details
devdash update <id> --status=in_progress   # Claim work
# ... do the work ...
devdash close <id>                         # Mark complete
```

### Creating tasks

```bash
devdash create --title="Fix login bug" --type=bug --priority=1
devdash create --title="Add dark mode" --type=feature --description="Support system theme"
```

### Managing dependencies

```bash
devdash create --title="Write tests" --type=task
devdash dep add <test-id> <feature-id>   # Tests depend on feature
devdash blocked                           # See what's waiting
```

## All Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `login` | Authenticate via browser (tries ports 18787-18792) |
| `init [name-or-id]` | Link repo to a project (auto-detect, name, ID prefix, or interactive picker) |
| `project create` | Create a new project (`--name`, `--repo`, `--description`) |
| `project list` | List all your projects |
| `project delete <id>` | Delete a project (`--force` to skip confirmation) |
| `list [--status=X] [--since=X]` | List tasks (optional status and time filter) |
| `ready [--since=X]` | Tasks with no blockers, sorted by automability then priority |
| `blocked` | Tasks waiting on dependencies |
| `stale [--since=X]` | In-progress tasks with no recent activity |
| `show <id>` | Task details with dependencies |
| `create --title="..."` | Create task (`--type`, `--priority`, `--description`, `--parent`) |
| `update <id> --key=val` | Update fields (`--status`, `--priority`, `--owner`, `--title`, `--description`, `--pre-instructions`) |
| `close <id> [...] [--pr=URL] [--commit=SHA] [--summary="..."]` | Close one or more tasks (with optional completion metadata) |
| `delete <id> [--force] [--cascade]` | Delete a task (`--cascade` deletes children) |
| `dep add <id> <dep-id>` | Add dependency (id depends on dep-id) |
| `dep remove <id> <dep-id>` | Remove dependency |
| `comment <id> --body="..."` | Add a comment to a task |
| `comments <id>` | List comments on a task |
| `activity [--since=X]` | View activity log |
| `report <id> --status=X` | Log agent progress (`code_complete\|committed\|pushed\|error`) |
| `find <uuid>` | Cross-project task lookup |
| `diagnose <id>` | Investigate task: status, job history, failure details |
| `jobs [--bead=<id>]` | List recent jobs (optionally filtered by task) |
| `reconcile` | AI-powered backlog audit |
| `score <id>` | Score task automability |
| `analyze <id>` | Analyze a task |
| `dispatch <id>` | Dispatch work to an agent |
| `sync` | Trigger full reconcile with GitHub |
| `import <num> \| --all` | Import GitHub issue(s) into the project |
| `stats` | Project statistics |
| `prime` | Output workflow context for AI agents (includes project health) |
| `doctor` | Check prerequisites and configuration |
| `agent-setup` | Configure AI agent instructions (`--agent=X`, `--all`, `--force`) |
| `alias-setup` | Add `dd` shortcut alias |
| `team` | List project team members |
| `token create\|list\|revoke` | Manage API tokens |
| `self-update` | Update to latest version |
| `uninstall` | Remove the CLI |

### Bead IDs

Commands accept UUIDs, short prefixes (e.g., `27bf`), or local IDs. Ambiguous prefixes are rejected with suggestions.

### dd alias

During `devdash init`, you'll be prompted to alias `dd` to `devdash` for shorter commands. This shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (Unix disk copy) — only enable if you don't use that tool.

```bash
devdash alias-setup
```

### Agent integration

`devdash init` detects AI agent configs (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/, AGENTS.md, etc.) and offers to run `agent-setup` automatically. You can also run it directly:

```bash
devdash agent-setup              # Interactive — detects your agents
devdash agent-setup --agent=claude,codex
devdash agent-setup --all --force
```

The `prime` command outputs structured context for AI coding agents:

```bash
devdash prime
```

It includes project name, health stats, command reference, and workflow patterns. Use it as a session hook to inject workflow context automatically.

### Multi-project setup

Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file linking it to a project. The CLI reads this file to determine which project to use:

```bash
cd my-project && devdash init    # Links this repo
cd other-repo && devdash init    # Links that repo separately
```

You can also set `DD_PROJECT_ID` and `DD_API_URL` environment variables to override, or use the `--project` flag on any command.

## Troubleshooting

| Issue | Fix |
|-------|-----|
| `Not logged in` | Run `devdash login` |
| `No project configured` | Run `devdash init` in your repo |
| `Port 18787 in use` during login | CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792 |
| `API error (401)` | Token expired — re-run `devdash login` |
| Commands seem slow | API latency — ensure good network connection |

Run `devdash doctor` to check all prerequisites and configuration.

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | User error (bad arguments, unknown command) |
| 2 | API error (network, auth, server error) |
| 3 | Config error (not logged in, no project) |

## Platforms

Pre-built binaries are available for:

- macOS (Apple Silicon and Intel)
- Linux (amd64 and arm64)
- Windows (amd64 and arm64)

## License

MIT

content.content:
1	# Getting Started with Dev-Dash
2	
3	Welcome! This guide will get you from zero to managing tasks in about 5 minutes.
4	
5	## What is Dev-Dash?
6	
7	Dev-Dash is a task tracker built for developers who work with AI coding agents. It has two parts:
8	
9	- **Web dashboard** at [dev-dash-blue.vercel.app](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app) — create projects, manage your team, connect GitHub, dispatch AI agents
10	- **CLI** (`devdash`) — manage tasks without leaving your terminal
11	
12	They stay in sync automatically. Use whichever feels right for the moment.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Step 1: Install the CLI
17	
18	```bash
19	curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
20	```
21	
22	Or via npm:
23	
24	```bash
25	npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
26	```
27	
28	Verify it worked:
29	
30	```bash
31	devdash version
32	```
33	
34	If anything looks off, run `devdash doctor` — it'll tell you exactly what's missing.
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Step 2: Log in
39	
40	```bash
41	devdash login
42	```
43	
44	Your browser will open for Google sign-in. After you authenticate, the auth token is saved locally and you're done — no passwords to remember, no keys to paste.
45	
46	> **First time?** If you don't have a Dev-Dash account yet, one is created automatically when you sign in with Google. No separate signup required.
47	
48	---
49	
50	## Step 3: Create or connect a project
51	
52	### Option A: Start from the web (recommended for new projects)
53	
54	1. Head to [dev-dash-blue.vercel.app](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app) and sign in
55	2. You'll land on the **onboarding page** — click **Connect GitHub**
56	3. Pick a repo from the list (or enter a project name manually)
57	4. Hit **Create Project**
58	
59	Dev-Dash can scan your GitHub issues and pull them in as tasks, but sometimes it's nice to have a clean slate 😁. Now that you have a project, let's link up your local work. 
60	
61	In your terminal, navigate to the root of the repo you want to work in:
62	
63	```bash
64	devdash init
65	```
66	
67	`init` auto-detects your GitHub remote (case-insensitive) and links the CLI to your project. If auto-detect fails, you'll see a numbered list to pick from — or you can pass a name or ID directly:
68	
69	```bash
70	devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
71	devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix
72	devdash init <full-uuid>        # Match by exact UUID
73	```
74	
75	A small `.devdash` file is created — commit it so teammates can use the CLI too.
76	
77	### Option B: Start from the CLI
78	
79	If you'd rather skip the browser:
80	
81	```bash
82	devdash project create --name="My Project" --repo=owner/repo
83	devdash init
84	```
85	
86	You can always connect GitHub and configure team settings from the web dashboard later.
87	
88	---
89	
90	## Step 4: Set up the shortcut (optional)
91	
92	During `init`, you'll be asked if you want to alias `dd` to `devdash`. If you said no (or missed it), you can do it anytime:
93	
94	```bash
95	devdash alias-setup
96	```
97	
98	From here on, the examples use `dd`. If you skipped the alias, substitute `devdash` everywhere.
99	
100	> **Heads up:** The alias installation shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (a Unix disk-copy utility). If you use that tool regularly, skip the alias and use the full name `devdash`.
101	
102	---
103	
104	## Step 5: Configure your AI agents (optional)
105	
106	If you use AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Cline), you can auto-generate config files so they know to use `dd` for task tracking.
107	
108	`devdash init` will detect agent configs and offer to run this during setup. You can also run it directly:
109	
110	```bash
111	dd agent-setup
112	```
113	
114	This auto-detects which agents you use and writes the appropriate config files. You can also specify agents directly:
115	
116	```bash
117	dd agent-setup --agent=claude,codex    # Just these two
118	dd agent-setup --all                   # All supported agents
119	dd agent-setup --all --force           # Overwrite existing configs
120	```
121	
122	Each agent gets instructions telling it to use `dd` commands, run `dd prime` at session start, and follow the git-push-before-close workflow.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## Step 6: Explore your project
127	
128	See what's ready to work on:
129	
130	```bash
131	dd ready
132	```
133	
134	This shows all unblocked tasks sorted by automability then priority. Pick one and dig in:
135	
136	```bash
137	dd show <id>
138	```
139	
140	You can use the full UUID, a short prefix (like `27bf`), or a local ID. The CLI figures it out.
141	
142	---
143	
144	## Your first workflow
145	
146	Here's the day-to-day loop:
147	
148	```bash
149	# What needs doing?
150	dd ready
151	
152	# Claim a task
153	dd update <id> --status=in_progress
154	
155	# ... write code, fix bugs, ship features ...
156	
157	# Done!
158	dd close <id>
159	```
160	
161	### Create a task
162	
163	```bash
164	dd create --title="Fix login redirect" --type=bug --priority=1
165	```
166	
167	Priority runs from 0 (critical) to 4 (backlog). Type can be `task`, `bug`, `feature`, or `enhancement`.
168	
169	### Add a dependency
170	
171	Some things need to happen in order:
172	
173	```bash
174	dd create --title="Write API endpoint" --type=task
175	dd create --title="Write tests for endpoint" --type=task
176	dd dep add <tests-id> <endpoint-id>
177	```
178	
179	Now the tests task won't show up in `dd ready` until the endpoint task is closed.
180	
181	### Check the big picture
182	
183	```bash
184	dd stats
185	```
186	
187	```
188	Total:       42
189	Pending:     28
190	In Progress: 3
191	Completed:   11
192	Blocked:     7
193	Ready:       21
194	```
195	
196	---
197	
198	## The web dashboard
199	
200	The CLI handles task management, but the dashboard gives you a few extras:
201	
202	| Feature | Where to find it |
203	|---------|-----------------|
204	| **Kanban board** | Project > Board tab |
205	| **AI agent dispatch** | Project > Agents tab |
206	| **GitHub sync settings** | Project > Settings tab |
207	| **Team invitations** | Project > Settings > Members |
208	| **API keys** (Anthropic, OpenAI) | Settings page (top-right menu) |
209	| **GitHub connection** | Settings > GitHub |
210	
211	To invite a teammate, go to your project settings and add their email. When they sign into Dev-Dash with Google, they'll automatically get access to your project, following the permissions you specify.
212	
213	---
214	
215	## Working with AI agents
216	
217	Dev-Dash is designed to work alongside AI coding agents like Claude Code. *Any agent* that can run a CLI command can work with Dev-Dash to create and retrieve issues in real time. Tell your agent to use `devdash` to create an issue for you, or retrieve details about existing issues in your projects. Dev-Dash is designed to work with agents directly, and the CLI installation (see `agent-setup`) helps your agent understand how to use `devdash` commands effectively.
218	
219	### Inject context about Dev-Dash and the current project
220	
221	```bash
222	dd prime
223	```
224	
225	This outputs a structured block of context — your project name, health stats, available commands, and workflow patterns. Set it up as a session hook so your agent always knows how to use `dd`.
226	
227	If in doubt, tell the agent to run `dd prime` and `dd help`.
228	
229	### Dispatch agents from the dashboard
230	
231	In the **Agents** tab, you can assign a task to an AI agent and watch it work. The agent gets your repo, the task description, and project context, then runs in a sandboxed environment.
232	
233	Check on agent jobs from the CLI:
234	
235	```bash
236	dd jobs                  # Recent runs
237	dd jobs --bead=<id>      # Jobs for a specific task
238	dd diagnose <id>         # Investigate a task: status, jobs, failures
239	```
240	
241	---
242	
243	## Multi-repo setup
244	
245	Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file. Just run `init` in each one:
246	
247	```bash
248	cd ~/projects/frontend && dd init
249	cd ~/projects/backend && dd init
250	cd ~/projects/infra && dd init
251	```
252	
253	The CLI reads `.devdash` to know which project you're working in. No global state to juggle.
254	
255	---
256	
257	## Quick reference
258	
259	| Command | What it does |
260	|---------|-------------|
261	| `dd login` | Sign in via browser |
262	| `dd init` | Link repo to project |
263	| `dd ready` | Show unblocked tasks |
264	| `dd list` | All open tasks |
265	| `dd show <id>` | Task details |
266	| `dd create --title="..."` | New task |
267	| `dd update <id> --status=in_progress` | Claim work |
268	| `dd close <id>` | Mark done |
269	| `dd dep add <a> <b>` | A depends on B |
270	| `dd blocked` | Show stuck tasks |
271	| `dd stale` | In-progress tasks with no activity |
272	| `dd diagnose <id>` | Investigate a task |
273	| `dd jobs` | Recent agent jobs |
274	| `dd stats` | Project health |
275	| `dd prime` | AI agent context |
276	| `dd agent-setup` | Configure AI agents |
277	| `dd doctor` | Check setup |
278	
279	---
280	
281	## Troubleshooting
282	
283	**"Not logged in"** — Run `dd login`.
284	
285	**"No project configured"** — Run `dd init` inside your repo.
286	
287	**Login port in use** — The CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792. If all are busy, free one up and retry.
288	
289	**Tasks not syncing with GitHub** — Check your GitHub connection in the web dashboard under Settings. You may need to re-authorize.
290	
291	When in doubt: `dd doctor` checks everything.
292	
293	---
294	
295	That's it! You're set up and ready to ship. Happy building.
296

# Getting Started with Dev-Dash

Welcome! This guide will get you from zero to managing tasks in about 5 minutes.

## What is Dev-Dash?

Dev-Dash is a task tracker built for developers who work with AI coding agents. It has two parts:

- **Web dashboard** at [dev-dash-blue.vercel.app](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app) — create projects, manage your team, connect GitHub, dispatch AI agents
- **CLI** (`devdash`) — manage tasks without leaving your terminal

They stay in sync automatically. Use whichever feels right for the moment.

---

## Step 1: Install the CLI

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
```

Or via npm:

```bash
npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
```

Verify it worked:

```bash
devdash version
```

If anything looks off, run `devdash doctor` — it'll tell you exactly what's missing.

---

## Step 2: Log in

```bash
devdash login
```

Your browser will open for Google sign-in. After you authenticate, the auth token is saved locally and you're done — no passwords to remember, no keys to paste.

> **First time?** If you don't have a Dev-Dash account yet, one is created automatically when you sign in with Google. No separate signup required.

---

## Step 3: Create or connect a project

### Option A: Start from the web (recommended for new projects)

1. Head to [dev-dash-blue.vercel.app](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app) and sign in
2. You'll land on the **onboarding page** — click **Connect GitHub**
3. Pick a repo from the list (or enter a project name manually)
4. Hit **Create Project**

Dev-Dash can scan your GitHub issues and pull them in as tasks, but sometimes it's nice to have a clean slate 😁. Now that you have a project, let's link up your local work. 

In your terminal, navigate to the root of the repo you want to work in:

```bash
devdash init
```

`init` auto-detects your GitHub remote (case-insensitive) and links the CLI to your project. If auto-detect fails, you'll see a numbered list to pick from — or you can pass a name or ID directly:

```bash
devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix
devdash init <full-uuid>        # Match by exact UUID
```

A small `.devdash` file is created — commit it so teammates can use the CLI too.

### Option B: Start from the CLI

If you'd rather skip the browser:

```bash
devdash project create --name="My Project" --repo=owner/repo
devdash init
```

You can always connect GitHub and configure team settings from the web dashboard later.

---

## Step 4: Set up the shortcut (optional)

During `init`, you'll be asked if you want to alias `dd` to `devdash`. If you said no (or missed it), you can do it anytime:

```bash
devdash alias-setup
```

From here on, the examples use `dd`. If you skipped the alias, substitute `devdash` everywhere.

> **Heads up:** The alias installation shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (a Unix disk-copy utility). If you use that tool regularly, skip the alias and use the full name `devdash`.

---

## Step 5: Configure your AI agents (optional)

If you use AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Cline), you can auto-generate config files so they know to use `dd` for task tracking.

`devdash init` will detect agent configs and offer to run this during setup. You can also run it directly:

```bash
dd agent-setup
```

This auto-detects which agents you use and writes the appropriate config files. You can also specify agents directly:

```bash
dd agent-setup --agent=claude,codex    # Just these two
dd agent-setup --all                   # All supported agents
dd agent-setup --all --force           # Overwrite existing configs
```

Each agent gets instructions telling it to use `dd` commands, run `dd prime` at session start, and follow the git-push-before-close workflow.

---

## Step 6: Explore your project

See what's ready to work on:

```bash
dd ready
```

This shows all unblocked tasks sorted by automability then priority. Pick one and dig in:

```bash
dd show <id>
```

You can use the full UUID, a short prefix (like `27bf`), or a local ID. The CLI figures it out.

---

## Your first workflow

Here's the day-to-day loop:

```bash
# What needs doing?
dd ready

# Claim a task
dd update <id> --status=in_progress

# ... write code, fix bugs, ship features ...

# Done!
dd close <id>
```

### Create a task

```bash
dd create --title="Fix login redirect" --type=bug --priority=1
```

Priority runs from 0 (critical) to 4 (backlog). Type can be `task`, `bug`, `feature`, or `enhancement`.

### Add a dependency

Some things need to happen in order:

```bash
dd create --title="Write API endpoint" --type=task
dd create --title="Write tests for endpoint" --type=task
dd dep add <tests-id> <endpoint-id>
```

Now the tests task won't show up in `dd ready` until the endpoint task is closed.

### Check the big picture

```bash
dd stats
```

```
Total:       42
Pending:     28
In Progress: 3
Completed:   11
Blocked:     7
Ready:       21
```

---

## The web dashboard

The CLI handles task management, but the dashboard gives you a few extras:

| Feature | Where to find it |
|---------|-----------------|
| **Kanban board** | Project > Board tab |
| **AI agent dispatch** | Project > Agents tab |
| **GitHub sync settings** | Project > Settings tab |
| **Team invitations** | Project > Settings > Members |
| **API keys** (Anthropic, OpenAI) | Settings page (top-right menu) |
| **GitHub connection** | Settings > GitHub |

To invite a teammate, go to your project settings and add their email. When they sign into Dev-Dash with Google, they'll automatically get access to your project, following the permissions you specify.

---

## Working with AI agents

Dev-Dash is designed to work alongside AI coding agents like Claude Code. *Any agent* that can run a CLI command can work with Dev-Dash to create and retrieve issues in real time. Tell your agent to use `devdash` to create an issue for you, or retrieve details about existing issues in your projects. Dev-Dash is designed to work with agents directly, and the CLI installation (see `agent-setup`) helps your agent understand how to use `devdash` commands effectively.

### Inject context about Dev-Dash and the current project

```bash
dd prime
```

This outputs a structured block of context — your project name, health stats, available commands, and workflow patterns. Set it up as a session hook so your agent always knows how to use `dd`.

If in doubt, tell the agent to run `dd prime` and `dd help`.

### Dispatch agents from the dashboard

In the **Agents** tab, you can assign a task to an AI agent and watch it work. The agent gets your repo, the task description, and project context, then runs in a sandboxed environment.

Check on agent jobs from the CLI:

```bash
dd jobs                  # Recent runs
dd jobs --bead=<id>      # Jobs for a specific task
dd diagnose <id>         # Investigate a task: status, jobs, failures
```

---

## Multi-repo setup

Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file. Just run `init` in each one:

```bash
cd ~/projects/frontend && dd init
cd ~/projects/backend && dd init
cd ~/projects/infra && dd init
```

The CLI reads `.devdash` to know which project you're working in. No global state to juggle.

---

## Quick reference

| Command | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| `dd login` | Sign in via browser |
| `dd init` | Link repo to project |
| `dd ready` | Show unblocked tasks |
| `dd list` | All open tasks |
| `dd show <id>` | Task details |
| `dd create --title="..."` | New task |
| `dd update <id> --status=in_progress` | Claim work |
| `dd close <id>` | Mark done |
| `dd dep add <a> <b>` | A depends on B |
| `dd blocked` | Show stuck tasks |
| `dd stale` | In-progress tasks with no activity |
| `dd diagnose <id>` | Investigate a task |
| `dd jobs` | Recent agent jobs |
| `dd stats` | Project health |
| `dd prime` | AI agent context |
| `dd agent-setup` | Configure AI agents |
| `dd doctor` | Check setup |

---

## Troubleshooting

**"Not logged in"** — Run `dd login`.

**"No project configured"** — Run `dd init` inside your repo.

**Login port in use** — The CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792. If all are busy, free one up and retry.

**Tasks not syncing with GitHub** — Check your GitHub connection in the web dashboard under Settings. You may need to re-authorize.

When in doubt: `dd doctor` checks everything.

---

That's it! You're set up and ready to ship. Happy building.

#!/bin/sh
set -e

# DevDash CLI installer
# Usage: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh

REPO="jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go"
INSTALL_DIR="${DEVDASH_INSTALL_DIR:-$HOME/.local/bin}"
BINARY="devdash"

# Detect platform
OS="$(uname -s | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')"
ARCH="$(uname -m)"

case "$ARCH" in
  x86_64|amd64) ARCH="amd64" ;;
  arm64|aarch64) ARCH="arm64" ;;
  *)
    echo "Error: unsupported architecture: $ARCH"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

case "$OS" in
  darwin|linux) EXT="tar.gz" ;;
  mingw*|msys*|cygwin*|windows*) OS="windows"; EXT="zip" ;;
  *)
    echo "Error: unsupported OS: $OS"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

# Get latest version
echo "Fetching latest version..."
VERSION=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/latest" | grep '"tag_name"' | sed -E 's/.*"v([^"]+)".*/\1/')

if [ -z "$VERSION" ]; then
  echo "Error: could not determine latest version"
  exit 1
fi

echo "Installing devdash v${VERSION} (${OS}/${ARCH})..."

# Download
ARCHIVE="devdash_${VERSION}_${OS}_${ARCH}.${EXT}"
URL="https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/v${VERSION}/${ARCHIVE}"

if [ "$OS" = "windows" ]; then
  BINARY="devdash.exe"
fi

TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap 'rm -rf "$TMPDIR"' EXIT

echo "Downloading $URL..."
curl -fsSL "$URL" -o "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE"

# Extract
if [ "$EXT" = "zip" ]; then
  unzip -q "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE" -d "$TMPDIR"
else
  tar -xzf "$TMPDIR/$ARCHIVE" -C "$TMPDIR"
fi

# Install
mkdir -p "$INSTALL_DIR"
cp "$TMPDIR/$BINARY" "$INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"
chmod +x "$INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"

echo "Installed to $INSTALL_DIR/$BINARY"

# Check PATH
case ":$PATH:" in
  *":$INSTALL_DIR:"*) ;;
  *)
    echo ""
    echo "Note: $INSTALL_DIR is not in your PATH."
    echo "Add it with:"
    echo "  export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\""
    echo ""
    SHELL_NAME=$(basename "$SHELL")
    case "$SHELL_NAME" in
      zsh)  echo "  echo 'export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\"' >> ~/.zshrc" ;;
      bash) echo "  echo 'export PATH=\"$INSTALL_DIR:\$PATH\"' >> ~/.bashrc" ;;
      fish) echo "  fish_add_path $INSTALL_DIR" ;;
    esac
    ;;
esac

echo ""
echo "Run 'devdash version' to verify."

# devdash-cli-go

Dev-Dash CLI — AI-powered task tracking for coding agents.

A fast, single-binary CLI that wraps the [Dev-Dash](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app/) REST API for managing tasks, dependencies, and agent jobs from the terminal. Built in Go with zero runtime dependencies.

This CLI can be run in your terminal but is optimized for agents. Don't feel like you need to memorize the commands; your agent should be familiar enough!

**New here?** Check out the **[Getting Started guide](GETTING-STARTED.md)** for a friendly walkthrough, or read **[Understanding the Workflow](UNDERSTANDING-THE-WORKFLOW.md)** for the philosophy behind Dev-Dash.

## Install

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
```

Or via npm:

```bash
npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
```

Or download a binary directly from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/releases).

### Build from source

```bash
go build -o devdash ./cmd/devdash
```

## Setup

```bash
# 1. Authenticate (opens browser for Google OAuth)
devdash login

# 2. Link to a project
devdash init                    # Auto-detects from git remote
devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix

# 3. Verify
devdash doctor
```

During init you'll be offered to set up the `dd` alias and configure AI agent instructions.

## Quick Start

```bash
devdash ready                              # Show tasks ready to work
devdash show <id>                          # View task details
devdash update <id> --status=in_progress   # Claim work
# ... do the work ...
devdash close <id>                         # Mark complete
```

### Creating tasks

```bash
devdash create --title="Fix login bug" --type=bug --priority=1
devdash create --title="Add dark mode" --type=feature --description="Support system theme"
```

### Managing dependencies

```bash
devdash create --title="Write tests" --type=task
devdash dep add <test-id> <feature-id>   # Tests depend on feature
devdash blocked                           # See what's waiting
```

## All Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `login` | Authenticate via browser (tries ports 18787-18792) |
| `init [name-or-id]` | Link repo to a project (auto-detect, name, ID prefix, or interactive picker) |
| `project create` | Create a new project (`--name`, `--repo`, `--description`) |
| `project list` | List all your projects |
| `project delete <id>` | Delete a project (`--force` to skip confirmation) |
| `list [--status=X] [--since=X]` | List tasks (optional status and time filter) |
| `ready [--since=X]` | Tasks with no blockers, sorted by automability then priority |
| `blocked` | Tasks waiting on dependencies |
| `stale [--since=X]` | In-progress tasks with no recent activity |
| `show <id>` | Task details with dependencies |
| `create --title="..."` | Create task (`--type`, `--priority`, `--description`, `--parent`) |
| `update <id> --key=val` | Update fields (`--status`, `--priority`, `--owner`, `--title`, `--description`, `--pre-instructions`) |
| `close <id> [...] [--pr=URL] [--commit=SHA] [--summary="..."]` | Close one or more tasks (with optional completion metadata) |
| `delete <id> [--force] [--cascade]` | Delete a task (`--cascade` deletes children) |
| `dep add <id> <dep-id>` | Add dependency (id depends on dep-id) |
| `dep remove <id> <dep-id>` | Remove dependency |
| `comment <id> --body="..."` | Add a comment to a task |
| `comments <id>` | List comments on a task |
| `activity [--since=X]` | View activity log |
| `report <id> --status=X` | Log agent progress (`code_complete\|committed\|pushed\|error`) |
| `find <uuid>` | Cross-project task lookup |
| `diagnose <id>` | Investigate task: status, job history, failure details |
| `jobs [--bead=<id>]` | List recent jobs (optionally filtered by task) |
| `reconcile` | AI-powered backlog audit |
| `score <id>` | Score task automability |
| `analyze <id>` | Analyze a task |
| `dispatch <id>` | Dispatch work to an agent |
| `sync` | Trigger full reconcile with GitHub |
| `import <num> \| --all` | Import GitHub issue(s) into the project |
| `stats` | Project statistics |
| `prime` | Output workflow context for AI agents (includes project health) |
| `doctor` | Check prerequisites and configuration |
| `agent-setup` | Configure AI agent instructions (`--agent=X`, `--all`, `--force`) |
| `alias-setup` | Add `dd` shortcut alias |
| `team` | List project team members |
| `token create\|list\|revoke` | Manage API tokens |
| `self-update` | Update to latest version |
| `uninstall` | Remove the CLI |

### Bead IDs

Commands accept UUIDs, short prefixes (e.g., `27bf`), or local IDs. Ambiguous prefixes are rejected with suggestions.

### dd alias

During `devdash init`, you'll be prompted to alias `dd` to `devdash` for shorter commands. This shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (Unix disk copy) — only enable if you don't use that tool.

```bash
devdash alias-setup
```

### Agent integration

`devdash init` detects AI agent configs (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/, AGENTS.md, etc.) and offers to run `agent-setup` automatically. You can also run it directly:

```bash
devdash agent-setup              # Interactive — detects your agents
devdash agent-setup --agent=claude,codex
devdash agent-setup --all --force
```

The `prime` command outputs structured context for AI coding agents:

```bash
devdash prime
```

It includes project name, health stats, command reference, and workflow patterns. Use it as a session hook to inject workflow context automatically.

### Multi-project setup

Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file linking it to a project. The CLI reads this file to determine which project to use:

```bash
cd my-project && devdash init    # Links this repo
cd other-repo && devdash init    # Links that repo separately
```

You can also set `DD_PROJECT_ID` and `DD_API_URL` environment variables to override, or use the `--project` flag on any command.

## Troubleshooting

| Issue | Fix |
|-------|-----|
| `Not logged in` | Run `devdash login` |
| `No project configured` | Run `devdash init` in your repo |
| `Port 18787 in use` during login | CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792 |
| `API error (401)` | Token expired — re-run `devdash login` |
| Commands seem slow | API latency — ensure good network connection |

Run `devdash doctor` to check all prerequisites and configuration.

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | User error (bad arguments, unknown command) |
| 2 | API error (network, auth, server error) |
| 3 | Config error (not logged in, no project) |

## Platforms

Pre-built binaries are available for:

- macOS (Apple Silicon and Intel)
- Linux (amd64 and arm64)
- Windows (amd64 and arm64)

## License

MIT

# Getting Started with Dev-Dash

Welcome! This guide will get you from zero to managing tasks in about 5 minutes.

## What is Dev-Dash?

Dev-Dash is a task tracker built for developers who work with AI coding agents. It has two parts:

- **Web dashboard** at [dev-dash-blue.vercel.app](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app) — create projects, manage your team, connect GitHub, dispatch AI agents
- **CLI** (`devdash`) — manage tasks without leaving your terminal

They stay in sync automatically. Use whichever feels right for the moment.

---

## Step 1: Install the CLI

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jasonmassey/devdash-cli-go/main/install.sh | sh
```

Or via npm:

```bash
npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
```

Verify it worked:

```bash
devdash version
```

If anything looks off, run `devdash doctor` — it'll tell you exactly what's missing.

---

## Step 2: Log in

```bash
devdash login
```

Your browser will open for Google sign-in. After you authenticate, the auth token is saved locally and you're done — no passwords to remember, no keys to paste.

> **First time?** If you don't have a Dev-Dash account yet, one is created automatically when you sign in with Google. No separate signup required.

---

## Step 3: Create or connect a project

### Option A: Start from the web (recommended for new projects)

1. Head to [dev-dash-blue.vercel.app](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app) and sign in
2. You'll land on the **onboarding page** — click **Connect GitHub**
3. Pick a repo from the list (or enter a project name manually)
4. Hit **Create Project**

Dev-Dash can scan your GitHub issues and pull them in as tasks, but sometimes it's nice to have a clean slate 😁. Now that you have a project, let's link up your local work. 

In your terminal, navigate to the root of the repo you want to work in:

```bash
devdash init
```

`init` auto-detects your GitHub remote (case-insensitive) and links the CLI to your project. If auto-detect fails, you'll see a numbered list to pick from — or you can pass a name or ID directly:

```bash
devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix
devdash init <full-uuid>        # Match by exact UUID
```

A small `.devdash` file is created — commit it so teammates can use the CLI too.

### Option B: Start from the CLI

If you'd rather skip the browser:

```bash
devdash project create --name="My Project" --repo=owner/repo
devdash init
```

You can always connect GitHub and configure team settings from the web dashboard later.

---

## Step 4: Set up the shortcut (optional)

During `init`, you'll be asked if you want to alias `dd` to `devdash`. If you said no (or missed it), you can do it anytime:

```bash
devdash alias-setup
```

From here on, the examples use `dd`. If you skipped the alias, substitute `devdash` everywhere.

> **Heads up:** The alias installation shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (a Unix disk-copy utility). If you use that tool regularly, skip the alias and use the full name `devdash`.

---

## Step 5: Configure your AI agents (optional)

If you use AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Cline), you can auto-generate config files so they know to use `dd` for task tracking.

`devdash init` will detect agent configs and offer to run this during setup. You can also run it directly:

```bash
dd agent-setup
```

This auto-detects which agents you use and writes the appropriate config files. You can also specify agents directly:

```bash
dd agent-setup --agent=claude,codex    # Just these two
dd agent-setup --all                   # All supported agents
dd agent-setup --all --force           # Overwrite existing configs
```

Each agent gets instructions telling it to use `dd` commands, run `dd prime` at session start, and follow the git-push-before-close workflow.

---

## Step 6: Explore your project

See what's ready to work on:

```bash
dd ready
```

This shows all unblocked tasks sorted by automability then priority. Pick one and dig in:

```bash
dd show <id>
```

You can use the full UUID, a short prefix (like `27bf`), or a local ID. The CLI figures it out.

---

## Your first workflow

Here's the day-to-day loop:

```bash
# What needs doing?
dd ready

# Claim a task
dd update <id> --status=in_progress

# ... write code, fix bugs, ship features ...

# Done!
dd close <id>
```

### Create a task

```bash
dd create --title="Fix login redirect" --type=bug --priority=1
```

Priority runs from 0 (critical) to 4 (backlog). Type can be `task`, `bug`, `feature`, or `enhancement`.

### Add a dependency

Some things need to happen in order:

```bash
dd create --title="Write API endpoint" --type=task
dd create --title="Write tests for endpoint" --type=task
dd dep add <tests-id> <endpoint-id>
```

Now the tests task won't show up in `dd ready` until the endpoint task is closed.

### Check the big picture

```bash
dd stats
```

```
Total:       42
Pending:     28
In Progress: 3
Completed:   11
Blocked:     7
Ready:       21
```

---

## The web dashboard

The CLI handles task management, but the dashboard gives you a few extras:

| Feature | Where to find it |
|---------|-----------------|
| **Kanban board** | Project > Board tab |
| **AI agent dispatch** | Project > Agents tab |
| **GitHub sync settings** | Project > Settings tab |
| **Team invitations** | Project > Settings > Members |
| **API keys** (Anthropic, OpenAI) | Settings page (top-right menu) |
| **GitHub connection** | Settings > GitHub |

To invite a teammate, go to your project settings and add their email. When they sign into Dev-Dash with Google, they'll automatically get access to your project, following the permissions you specify.

---

## Working with AI agents

Dev-Dash is designed to work alongside AI coding agents like Claude Code. *Any agent* that can run a CLI command can work with Dev-Dash to create and retrieve issues in real time. Tell your agent to use `devdash` to create an issue for you, or retrieve details about existing issues in your projects. Dev-Dash is designed to work with agents directly, and the CLI installation (see `agent-setup`) helps your agent understand how to use `devdash` commands effectively.

### Inject context about Dev-Dash and the current project

```bash
dd prime
```

This outputs a structured block of context — your project name, health stats, available commands, and workflow patterns. Set it up as a session hook so your agent always knows how to use `dd`.

If in doubt, tell the agent to run `dd prime` and `dd help`.

### Dispatch agents from the dashboard

In the **Agents** tab, you can assign a task to an AI agent and watch it work. The agent gets your repo, the task description, and project context, then runs in a sandboxed environment.

Check on agent jobs from the CLI:

```bash
dd jobs                  # Recent runs
dd jobs --bead=<id>      # Jobs for a specific task
dd diagnose <id>         # Investigate a task: status, jobs, failures
```

---

## Multi-repo setup

Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file. Just run `init` in each one:

```bash
cd ~/projects/frontend && dd init
cd ~/projects/backend && dd init
cd ~/projects/infra && dd init
```

The CLI reads `.devdash` to know which project you're working in. No global state to juggle.

---

## Quick reference

| Command | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| `dd login` | Sign in via browser |
| `dd init` | Link repo to project |
| `dd ready` | Show unblocked tasks |
| `dd list` | All open tasks |
| `dd show <id>` | Task details |
| `dd create --title="..."` | New task |
| `dd update <id> --status=in_progress` | Claim work |
| `dd close <id>` | Mark done |
| `dd dep add <a> <b>` | A depends on B |
| `dd blocked` | Show stuck tasks |
| `dd stale` | In-progress tasks with no activity |
| `dd diagnose <id>` | Investigate a task |
| `dd jobs` | Recent agent jobs |
| `dd stats` | Project health |
| `dd prime` | AI agent context |
| `dd agent-setup` | Configure AI agents |
| `dd doctor` | Check setup |

---

## Troubleshooting

**"Not logged in"** — Run `dd login`.

**"No project configured"** — Run `dd init` inside your repo.

**Login port in use** — The CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792. If all are busy, free one up and retry.

**Tasks not syncing with GitHub** — Check your GitHub connection in the web dashboard under Settings. You may need to re-authorize.

When in doubt: `dd doctor` checks everything.

---

That's it! You're set up and ready to ship. Happy building.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# devdash-cli-go

Dev-Dash CLI — AI-powered task tracking for coding agents.

A fast, single-binary CLI that wraps the [Dev-Dash](https://dev-dash-blue.vercel.app/) REST API for managing tasks, dependencies, and agent jobs from the terminal. Built in Go with zero runtime dependencies.

This CLI can be run in your terminal but is optimized for agents. Don't feel like you need to memorize the commands; your agent should be familiar enough!

**New here?** Check out the **[Getting Started guide](GETTING-STARTED.md)** for a friendly walkthrough, or read **[Understanding the Workflow](UNDERSTANDING-THE-WORKFLOW.md)** for the philosophy behind Dev-Dash.

## Install

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/main/install.sh | sh
```

Or via npm:

```bash
npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
```

Or download a binary directly from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/releases).

### Build from source

```bash
go build -o devdash ./cmd/devdash
```

## Setup

```bash
# 1. Authenticate (opens browser for Google OAuth)
devdash login

# 2. Link to a project
devdash init                    # Auto-detects from git remote
devdash init MyProject          # Match by name (case-insensitive)
devdash init 896b3dbc           # Match by ID prefix

# 3. Verify
devdash doctor
```

During init you'll be offered to set up the `dd` alias and configure AI agent instructions.

## Quick Start

```bash
devdash ready                              # Show tasks ready to work
devdash show <id>                          # View task details
devdash update <id> --status=in_progress   # Claim work
# ... do the work ...
devdash close <id>                         # Mark complete
```

### Creating tasks

```bash
devdash create --title="Fix login bug" --type=bug --priority=1
devdash create --title="Add dark mode" --type=feature --description="Support system theme"
```

### Managing dependencies

```bash
devdash create --title="Write tests" --type=task
devdash dep add <test-id> <feature-id>   # Tests depend on feature
devdash blocked                           # See what's waiting
```

## All Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `login` | Authenticate via browser (tries ports 18787-18792) |
| `init [name-or-id]` | Link repo to a project (auto-detect, name, ID prefix, or interactive picker) |
| `project create` | Create a new project (`--name`, `--repo`, `--description`) |
| `project list` | List all your projects |
| `project delete <id>` | Delete a project (`--force` to skip confirmation) |
| `list [--status=X] [--since=X]` | List tasks (optional status and time filter) |
| `ready [--since=X]` | Tasks with no blockers, sorted by automability then priority |
| `blocked` | Tasks waiting on dependencies |
| `stale [--since=X]` | In-progress tasks with no recent activity |
| `show <id>` | Task details with dependencies |
| `create --title="..."` | Create task (`--type`, `--priority`, `--description`, `--parent`) |
| `update <id> --key=val` | Update fields (`--status`, `--priority`, `--owner`, `--title`, `--description`, `--pre-instructions`) |
| `close <id> [...] [--pr=URL] [--commit=SHA] [--summary="..."]` | Close one or more tasks (with optional completion metadata) |
| `delete <id> [--force] [--cascade]` | Delete a task (`--cascade` deletes children) |
| `dep add <id> <dep-id>` | Add dependency (id depends on dep-id) |
| `dep remove <id> <dep-id>` | Remove dependency |
| `comment <id> --body="..."` | Add a comment to a task |
| `comments <id>` | List comments on a task |
| `activity [--since=X]` | View activity log |
| `report <id> --status=X` | Log agent progress (`code_complete\|committed\|pushed\|error`) |
| `find <uuid>` | Cross-project task lookup |
| `diagnose <id>` | Investigate task: status, job history, failure details |
| `jobs [--bead=<id>]` | List recent jobs (optionally filtered by task) |
| `reconcile` | AI-powered backlog audit |
| `score <id>` | Score task automability |
| `analyze <id>` | Analyze a task |
| `dispatch <id>` | Dispatch work to an agent |
| `sync` | Trigger full reconcile with GitHub |
| `import <num> \| --all` | Import GitHub issue(s) into the project |
| `stats` | Project statistics |
| `prime` | Output workflow context for AI agents (includes project health) |
| `doctor` | Check prerequisites and configuration |
| `agent-setup` | Configure AI agent instructions (`--agent=X`, `--all`, `--force`) |
| `alias-setup` | Add `dd` shortcut alias |
| `team` | List project team members |
| `token create\|list\|revoke` | Manage API tokens |
| `self-update` | Update to latest version |
| `uninstall` | Remove the CLI |

### Bead IDs

Commands accept UUIDs, short prefixes (e.g., `27bf`), or local IDs. Ambiguous prefixes are rejected with suggestions.

### dd alias

During `devdash init`, you'll be prompted to alias `dd` to `devdash` for shorter commands. This shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (Unix disk copy) — only enable if you don't use that tool.

```bash
devdash alias-setup
```

### Agent integration

`devdash init` detects AI agent configs (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/, AGENTS.md, etc.) and offers to run `agent-setup` automatically. You can also run it directly:

```bash
devdash agent-setup              # Interactive — detects your agents
devdash agent-setup --agent=claude,codex
devdash agent-setup --all --force
```

The `prime` command outputs structured context for AI coding agents:

```bash
devdash prime
```

It includes project name, health stats, command reference, and workflow patterns. Use it as a session hook to inject workflow context automatically.

### Multi-project setup

Each repo gets its own `.devdash` file linking it to a project. The CLI reads this file to determine which project to use:

```bash
cd my-project && devdash init    # Links this repo
cd other-repo && devdash init    # Links that repo separately
```

You can also set `DD_PROJECT_ID` and `DD_API_URL` environment variables to override, or use the `--project` flag on any command.

## Troubleshooting

| Issue | Fix |
|-------|-----|
| `Not logged in` | Run `devdash login` |
| `No project configured` | Run `devdash init` in your repo |
| `Port 18787 in use` during login | CLI auto-tries ports 18787-18792 |
| `API error (401)` | Token expired — re-run `devdash login` |
| Commands seem slow | API latency — ensure good network connection |

Run `devdash doctor` to check all prerequisites and configuration.

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | User error (bad arguments, unknown command) |
| 2 | API error (network, auth, server error) |
| 3 | Config error (not logged in, no project) |

## Platforms

Pre-built binaries are available for:

- macOS (Apple Silicon and Intel)
- Linux (amd64 and arm64)
- Windows (amd64 and arm64)

## License

MIT

content.text:
Done. All references now point to `devdashproject/devdash-cli`. 

**Next up is Step 5 from the plan — you need to set up npm.**

1. **Create the `@devdashproject` npm org**: https://www.npmjs.com/org/create → name `devdashproject`, free tier
2. **Create an automation token**: https://www.npmjs.com/settings → Tokens → Generate New Token → choose **"Automation"** type (this bypasses 2FA for CI)
3. **Add `NPM_TOKEN` as a GitHub secret**: https://github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/settings/secrets/actions → New repository secret → name `NPM_TOKEN`, paste the token

Tell me when that's done and I'll fix the npm pipeline bugs (artifact passing, bin shim, build-packages.js).

message.content: what about token permissions for packages and scopes, and for organizations?

content.text:
Good question. Here's what needs to line up:

**npm org → packages:**
- When you create the `devdashproject` org, your npm account becomes an owner
- Owners can publish any package under the `@devdashproject` scope
- The free tier only allows public packages, which is what you want

**Token → scope:**
- When creating the automation token, npm will ask about permissions
- You need **"Read and write"** permission for packages
- The token should be scoped to the `@devdashproject` org (not all your packages, if you have others)
- If npm gives you the option to restrict to specific packages, skip it — you'll be publishing 7 packages (`devdash-cli` + 6 platform packages) and they don't exist yet

**GitHub Actions → token:**
- The `NPM_TOKEN` secret is a repo-level secret, only accessible to workflows in that repo
- The `actions/setup-node@v4` with `registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org` configures npm to use `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` from the environment, which the workflow sets to `secrets.NPM_TOKEN`

**One gotcha:** The first publish of a scoped package must use `--access public` (which the workflow already does). Scoped packages default to private, and private packages require a paid npm plan.

So the flow is:
1. Create org `devdashproject` on npmjs.com
2. Create automation token with **read-write** access, scoped to `@devdashproject`
3. Add as `NPM_TOKEN` in GitHub repo secrets

No special org-level settings needed beyond that. Does this clear things up, or do you have questions about any of these steps?

file.content:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version-file: go.mod

      - name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: latest
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

  npm-publish:
    needs: release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - name: Get version from tag
        id: version
        run: echo "VERSION=${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Build npm packages
        run: |
          cd npm
          node build-packages.js ${{ steps.version.outputs.VERSION }}

      - name: Publish platform packages
        run: |
          for dir in npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 npm/devdash-darwin-x64 npm/devdash-linux-x64 npm/devdash-linux-arm64 npm/devdash-win32-x64 npm/devdash-win32-arm64; do
            if [ -d "$dir" ]; then
              cd "$dir"
              npm publish --access public || true
              cd ../..
            fi
          done
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

      - name: Publish wrapper package
        run: |
          cd npm/devdash-cli
          npm publish --access public
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

input.content:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version-file: go.mod

      - name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: latest
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Upload dist for npm
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/
          retention-days: 1

  npm-publish:
    needs: release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - name: Download dist
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/

      - name: Get version from tag
        id: version
        run: echo "VERSION=${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Determine npm tag
        id: npm-tag
        run: |
          VERSION="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"
          if [[ "$VERSION" == *-* ]]; then
            echo "TAG=next" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          else
            echo "TAG=latest" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi

      - name: Build npm packages
        run: |
          cd npm
          node build-packages.js ${{ steps.version.outputs.VERSION }}

      - name: Publish platform packages
        run: |
          for dir in npm/devdash-darwin-arm64 npm/devdash-darwin-x64 npm/devdash-linux-x64 npm/devdash-linux-arm64 npm/devdash-win32-x64 npm/devdash-win32-arm64; do
            if [ -d "$dir" ]; then
              cd "$dir"
              npm publish --access public --tag ${{ steps.npm-tag.outputs.TAG }}
              cd ../..
            else
              echo "ERROR: $dir does not exist"
              exit 1
            fi
          done
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

      - name: Publish wrapper package
        run: |
          cd npm/devdash-cli
          npm publish --access public --tag ${{ steps.npm-tag.outputs.TAG }}
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

input.command: devdash close ea53 --summary="Fixed three bugs in npm release pipeline: (1) Added artifact upload/download between release and npm-publish jobs — dist/ was missing in the npm job, causing all 'binary not found' warnings. (2) Added Windows entries to bin shim (win32-x64, win32-arm64) and .exe handling. (3) Removed bin entries from platform packages so only the wrapper's JS shim defines the bin entry. Also added prerelease npm tag support (rc/beta → next tag) and replaced || true with proper error handling. PR: https://github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/pull/5" --commit=$(git rev-parse HEAD) --pr=https://github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/pull/5 2>&1

content.text:
Pipeline is fixed and merged. **Now it's time to test with a real release.**

The next step is to tag a prerelease and see if the full pipeline works end-to-end. This will:
1. GoReleaser builds binaries for all 6 platforms
2. Uploads dist/ as an artifact
3. npm-publish job downloads it, runs build-packages.js, publishes to npmjs.com

To trigger it, you'll need to tag and push from main:

```bash
git tag v0.4.0-rc.1
git push origin v0.4.0-rc.1
```

Since the tag needs to be on main (which has the merged PRs), I can't do it from this worktree. Want me to exit the worktree and tag from the main repo?

content.text: The directory `npm/devdash-darwin-arm64` exists (the `-d` check passed) but the `package.json` inside it doesn't. This means `build-packages.js` created the directory structure but the binary wasn't found, so `continue` skipped writing the package.json. Let me check what build-packages.js actually output:

content.content:
2026-04-15T02:34:07.0612029Z (node:2339) [DEP0005] DeprecationWarning: Buffer() is deprecated due to security and usability issues. Please use the Buffer.alloc(), Buffer.allocUnsafe(), or Buffer.from() methods instead.

content.text: So the amd64 builds were found but arm64 builds weren't. This is the GoReleaser v2 path naming issue — amd64 gets a `_v1` suffix (GOAMD64 version), arm64 doesn't get a suffix but also doesn't match the expected path. Let me check what the actual dist directory structure looks like:

input.description: Look for dist paths in logs

content.text:
There it is. The `build-packages.js` tries:
- `devdash_darwin_arm64` (doesn't exist)
- `devdash_darwin_arm64_v1` (doesn't exist — it's `_v8.0`)

GoReleaser v2 uses `_v1` for amd64 (GOAMD64=v1) and `_v8.0` for arm64 (GOARM64=v8.0). The script only tries unsuffixed and `_v1`. Let me fix `build-packages.js` to also try `_v8.0`:

content.content: The file /Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/npm/build-packages.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: -  // Find the binary from goreleaser output
+  // Find the binary from goreleaser output.
+  // GoReleaser v2 appends version suffixes: _v1 for amd64, _v8.0 for arm64.

input.command:
cd /Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go && git checkout npm/devdash-cli/package.json && git add npm/build-packages.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix GoReleaser v2 path lookup for arm64 binaries

GoReleaser v2 uses _v1 suffix for amd64 and _v8.0 for arm64.
build-packages.js only tried unsuffixed and _v1, so arm64 binaries
were never found. Now tries all three variants. Also changed from
warning+continue to error+exit on missing binaries so failures
aren't silent.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push 2>&1

content.text: The binary is there, nested inside `node_modules`. Let me test the shim:

content.content: devdash 0.3.2

content.text: Wait — that says 0.3.2 but the binary should be 0.4.0-rc.2. Let me check:

content.content: devdash 0.3.2

content.text: The binary itself reports 0.3.2 because GoReleaser detected the snapshot as `v0.4.0-rc.2` but the ldflags inject the version from the tag. This is actually the tag version — the `Version` variable defaults to `0.3.2` at compile time when set in root.go. Actually, let me re-check — GoReleaser should inject via ldflags:

input.command: gh release view v0.4.0-rc.2 --repo devdashproject/devdash-cli --json tagName,isPrerelease 2>&1

content.content: {"isPrerelease":true,"tagName":"v0.4.0-rc.2"}

toolUseResult.stdout: {"isPrerelease":true,"tagName":"v0.4.0-rc.2"}

content.content: 12:const Version = "0.3.2"

content.text:
It's a `const`, not a `var` — ldflags can't override constants. That's a separate bug but not blocking for the npm pipeline. The important thing is: **the npm install works end-to-end.** The shim finds the platform binary and executes it correctly.

Let me close the issue and report the status.

input.command: devdash close ea53 --summary="npm pipeline fully working. Fixed: (1) artifact passing between jobs, (2) GoReleaser v2 arm64 path lookup (_v8.0 suffix), (3) bin shim Windows entries, (4) removed bin entries from platform packages. v0.4.0-rc.2 published successfully to npmjs.com — all 6 platform packages + wrapper. npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli@0.4.0-rc.2 works end-to-end. Found separate bug: Version is const not var so ldflags can't inject — tracked separately." --commit=$(git rev-parse HEAD) 2>&1

content.text: Simple fix — change `const` to `var` so GoReleaser's ldflags can inject the version:

input.old_string: const Version = "0.3.2"
input.new_string: var Version = "0.3.2"

git add internal/commands/root.go && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix version injection: change Version from const to var

GoReleaser ldflags (-X ...commands.Version) can only override var,
not const. Version was showing 0.3.2 for all releases regardless
of the tag.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push 2>&1

content.content:
UNDERSTANDING-THE-WORKFLOW.md:27:- **With a coding agent:** The Dev-Dash CLI installs instructions that help your agent interact with Dev-Dash. When your agent is in plan mode, encourage it to utilize sub-issues and dependencies so that your team will have a full view of how the agent plans to proceed. The agent updates the task description with its analysis, making the plan visible to everyone.
UNDERSTANDING-THE-WORKFLOW.md:33:The installed agent instructions guide your coding agent to mark the task as in progress when work begins, and to close and summarize the work once it is complete. Since Dev-Dash is an external resource, a complex worktree can be followed by an agent even through session compaction or agent disconnects — or can be picked up by a different team member using a different agent.
README.md:11:## Install
README.md:14:curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/main/install.sh | sh
README.md:20:npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
README.md:114:| `uninstall` | Remove the CLI |
GETTING-STARTED.md:16:## Step 1: Install the CLI
GETTING-STARTED.md:19:curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/main/install.sh | sh
GETTING-STARTED.md:25:npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
GETTING-STARTED.md:100:> **Heads up:** The alias installation shadows `/usr/bin/dd` (a Unix disk-copy utility). If you use that tool regularly, skip the alias and use the full name `devdash`.
GETTING-STARTED.md:217:Dev-Dash is designed to work alongside AI coding agents like Claude Code. *Any agent* that can run a CLI command can work with Dev-Dash to create and retrieve issues in real time. Tell your agent to use `devdash` to create an issue for you, or retrieve details about existing issues in your projects. Dev-Dash is designed to work with agents directly, and the CLI installation (see `agent-setup`) helps your agent understand how to use `devdash` commands effectively.

file.content:
## Install

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/main/install.sh | sh
```

Or via npm:

```bash
npm install -g @devdashproject/devdash-cli
```

Or download a binary directly from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/devdashproject/devdash-cli/releases).

### Build from source

```bash
go build -o devdash ./cmd/devdash
```

content.text:
It's trying to upload to the **rc.3** release (ID 309133332) which already has assets from the earlier run. GoReleaser is seeing `v0.4.0` tag but uploading to the rc.3 release. This happened because there's no GitHub release for `v0.4.0` yet, and GoReleaser found the existing rc.3 release.

Let me delete the v0.4.0 tag, clean up, and retag:

input.command: git push origin :v0.4.0 2>&1 && git tag -d v0.4.0 2>&1

content.text: The issue is that GoReleaser might be confused by the rc tags. Let me check if there's a draft release left over:
No leftover draft. I think the problem was that GoReleaser saw it was the same commit as rc.3 and tried to update that release. Let me make a tiny commit so v0.4.0 is on a different commit:

content.content: 12	var Version = "0.3.2"

file.content: var Version = "0.3.2"

input.old_string: var Version = "0.3.2"
input.new_string: var Version = "0.4.0"

content.content: The file /Users/jason/playground/claudespace/devdash-cli-go/internal/commands/root.go has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

toolUseResult.oldString: var Version = "0.3.2"
toolUseResult.newString: var Version = "0.4.0"

lines: // Deps holds shared dependencies injected into all commands.

input.command:
git add internal/commands/root.go && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Bump default version to 0.4.0

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push 2>&1

content.text:
Everything is clean:

- **GitHub release**: v0.4.0, not prerelease, all 6 platform archives + checksums
- **npm**: `latest` tag points to `0.4.0`, `next` tag points to `0.4.0-rc.3`
- **curl installer**: will now fetch v0.4.0 (the new `/releases/latest`)

v0.4.0 is shipped across all channels.
